proposals.title: Disposable, High-Flux Facilitated Transport Membranes from Plasticized Commodity Polymers for Single-Use Biogas Upgrading
proposals.motivation: Biogas upgrading to biomethane via membrane separation is a key renewable energy technology, but capital cost remains a barrier for small-scale, distributed applications. Current polymeric membranes are engineered for long-term thermal and chemical stability, which is unnecessary for seasonal or single-use processing and adds significant material and fabrication expense. A paradigm shift toward low-cost, disposable membranes could dramatically improve process economics and accessibility.
proposals.hypothesis: That high-performance, CO2-selective facilitated transport membranes can be fabricated from low-cost, low-glass-transition-temperature (Tg) commodity polymers (e.g., polyethers, plasticized PVC) and mobile carriers, achieving sufficient selectivity and flux for short-lifetime biogas upgrading despite their inherent lack of long-term thermal stability.
proposals.method: We will fabricate membranes by solution-casting blends of commodity polymers (e.g., polyethylene oxide, PEO) with high concentrations of plasticizers (e.g., ionic liquids, poly(ethylene glycol)) and mobile CO2 carriers (e.g., amino acid salts). Sorption (gravimetric) and transport (time-lag, mixed-gas permeation) properties for CO2, CH4, N2, and H2S will be characterized. Performance will be evaluated under simulated biogas (55-65% CH4, 35-45% CO2) at ambient to 40°C. Membrane lifetime and performance decay will be actively monitored over a target 100-hour operational window.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design framework for 'fit-for-purpose,' disposable membranes, decoupling performance from longevity. It will provide a database of structure-property relationships for highly plasticized, carrier-filled systems relevant to aggressive, short-term gas separations. The results could enable new, low-capital business models for modular biogas upgrading.
proposals.novelty: It directly challenges the entrenched materials axiom that high stability is prerequisite for gas separation membranes, intentionally leveraging transient, non-equilibrium polymer states (high free volume, mobile carriers) that are typically avoided. The approach synergizes Freeman group expertise in ion pairing/sorption with subnanoconfined transport, but applies it to unstable, low-Tg matrices.

proposals.title: Crosslinked Polyimides with Engineered Sub-Nanoporosity for Enhanced Stability in Aggressive CO2/Hydrocarbon Gas Separations
proposals.motivation: Polyimide membranes for natural gas sweetening and biogas upgrading suffer from plasticization and physical aging when exposed to aggressive feeds containing condensable hydrocarbons (e.g., C3+) and high-pressure CO2. This leads to a severe, time-dependent loss of selectivity, increasing separation costs and limiting process reliability. Developing membranes resistant to these phenomena is critical for the economic deployment of membrane technology in real-world gas separations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating rigid, crosslinkable moieties derived from metal-organic framework (MOF) linker chemistry into polyimide backbones, followed by thermal crosslinking, will create a membrane with a rigid, size-selective sub-nanoporous structure that resists polymer chain rearrangement (aging) and swelling (plasticization) by aggressive penetrants.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel polyimides by incorporating rigid, angular diamine monomers with pendant crosslinkable groups (e.g., acetylene or maleimide) inspired by MOF linker design principles. Membranes will be cast and thermally crosslinked to form a robust network. Sorption and transport properties will be characterized using high-pressure gravimetric sorption for CO2, CH4, and n-C4H10, and time-lag permeation experiments. Long-term stability will be assessed via continuous mixed-gas (CO2/CH4/C3H8) permeation tests at elevated pressure, monitoring permeability and selectivity over 100+ hours.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-property relationship between rigid, crosslinked polyimide architectures and their resistance to aging and plasticization. It will deliver a new membrane material platform with documented long-term performance data under aggressive conditions, directly informing the design of next-generation gas separation membranes. The study will also advance the understanding of sub-nanoscale pore engineering in polymers for controlling penetrant-induced swelling.
proposals.novelty: While crosslinking is known, the novelty lies in the deliberate design of the polyimide precursor using MOF-inspired rigidity and geometry to pre-organize the polymer matrix, creating a tailored, stable sub-nanoporosity post-crosslinking, rather than relying on crosslinking alone to lock a potentially non-ideal structure. This integrates concepts from ultra-confined ion channel design into bulk gas separation polymers.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Computational Screening of Polymer Chemical Space for Advanced Gas Separation Membranes
proposals.motivation: The development of polymer membranes for gas separations (e.g., CO2 capture, hydrogen purification) is limited by the vastness of chemical design space and the high cost of experimental synthesis and testing. Prior to 2026, while computational screening has been applied to porous crystals like MOFs, its systematic application to amorphous, solution-processable polymers remains underexplored. A predictive, high-throughput framework is needed to identify novel polymer structures with exceptional combinations of permeability and selectivity, accelerating the discovery of next-generation membrane materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating classical molecular dynamics simulations with graph neural network (GNN) models trained on polymer structural fingerprints, we can accurately predict gas solubility and diffusivity, enabling the virtual screening of thousands of hypothetical polymers to identify high-performance candidates for targeted gas pairs.
proposals.method: We will first curate and expand an open database of experimental gas permeability, diffusivity, and solubility for polymers. For a diverse set of candidate repeat units, we will use high-throughput molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with tools like LAMMPS and the TraPPE force field to compute key properties like fractional free volume and cohesive energy density. These MD-derived descriptors, along with molecular fingerprints (e.g., Morgan fingerprints), will be used to train a multi-task GNN architecture (e.g., using the DGL or PyTorch Geometric libraries) to predict gas permeability and selectivity. The trained model will screen a virtual library of polymers generated by combinatorially linking candidate repeat units.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly accessible, predictive model for polymer membrane performance, directly linking chemical structure to gas transport properties. It will yield a ranked list of promising, novel polymer structures for synthesis, providing specific targets for experimental validation. The integrated computational pipeline will establish a new methodology for rational polymer design in separations.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput screening is established for rigid, crystalline materials like MOFs, its application to amorphous polymers for gas separation is novel, requiring the prediction of solubility-diffusivity mechanisms rather than rigid pore metrics. The integration of physics-based MD simulations with data-driven GNNs specifically tailored for polymer repeat unit structures and topology represents a significant advance over prior quantitative structure-property relationship (QSPR) models that relied on simpler descriptors.

proposals.title: Precise Layer-by-Layer Assembly of Polyelectrolyte Complexes onto Porous Supports for High-Performance Nanofiltration Membranes
proposals.motivation: The development of highly selective, high-flux membranes for water purification and resource recovery is limited by the difficulty in creating defect-free, ultra-thin selective layers with controlled charge and pore architecture. Traditional interfacial polymerization offers limited control over layer thickness and chemistry. Layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly of polyelectrolytes is a versatile technique for creating tunable nanoscale films, but its transfer from model flat substrates to high-surface-area, porous supports for practical membranes remains a significant fabrication challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely controlling the ionic strength, pH, and deposition cycles during LbL assembly of polycations and polyanions directly onto engineered ultrafiltration supports, we can create sub-50 nm selective skins with tailored charge density and mesh size, enabling exceptional monovalent/divalent ion selectivity without sacrificing water permeability.
proposals.method: We will fabricate membranes by alternately depositing poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) and poly(sodium 4-styrenesulfonate) (PSS) from solutions with varying NaCl concentrations (0.1-1.0 M) onto polyethersulfone ultrafiltration supports. Deposition will be automated using a dip-coater. The nanostructure (thickness, roughness) will be characterized by ellipsometry and AFM. Membrane performance (water flux, NaCl/MgSO4 rejection) will be tested in cross-flow filtration. We will systematically correlate deposition conditions (salt concentration, bilayer number) with skin properties (swelling, charge) and separation performance, explicitly accounting for ion-pairing effects within the polyelectrolyte layers as per recent group insights.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust framework for translating LbL assembly into a practical membrane fabrication platform, providing clear structure-property-performance relationships for polyelectrolyte complex skins. It will contribute fundamental knowledge on ion and water transport in confined, charged polymeric layers and deliver a new class of tunable membranes for selective separations.
proposals.novelty: While LbL is well-studied on flat surfaces, its precise application to form defect-free, ultra-thin selective skins on porous supports for pressure-driven filtration is non-trivial. The novelty lies in the systematic integration of LbL processing parameters with the Freeman group's deep expertise in ion sorption and transport in polymers to directly engineer next-generation nanofiltration membranes.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Subnanoporous Polymer Membranes for High-Performance CO2/N2 Separation via Active Learning and Molecular Simulation
proposals.motivation: The development of polymer membranes for gas separations like carbon capture is traditionally Edisonian, requiring the synthesis and testing of numerous candidates. This process is slow and often fails to identify optimal materials. An inverse design approach, which computationally generates polymer structures to meet specific performance targets, could dramatically accelerate the discovery of next-generation membranes, a critical need for energy-efficient separations pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations with an active learning loop and a genetic algorithm, we can inversely design polymer repeat unit structures that self-assemble into membranes with subnanoscale channels possessing the precise size-sieving and sorption properties needed for high CO2/N2 selectivity and permeability.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline where a genetic algorithm generates candidate polymer repeat units. Their bulk membrane properties (e.g., fractional free volume, chain packing) and gas transport performance (solubility and diffusivity coefficients for CO2 and N2) will be predicted using coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations with validated force fields. An active learning loop, using a surrogate model (e.g., Gaussian Process Regression), will iteratively select promising candidates for simulation to update the model and guide the genetic algorithm towards polymers meeting a target Robeson upper-bound performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, generalizable computational framework for the inverse design of gas separation membranes. It will yield a set of new, high-performance polymer structures for CO2/N2 separation, providing fundamental insights into the molecular features that govern selective transport in subnanoscale polymer channels.
proposals.novelty: While inverse design has been explored for discrete molecules, its application to designing bulk polymer membrane materials—where performance depends on emergent, self-assembled nanostructure—is a significant and non-obvious advance. This approach directly bridges molecular-scale design with macroscopic transport properties.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Nanofiller Geometry and Chemistry on Polymer Chain Packing and Free Volume in Mixed-Matrix Membranes
proposals.motivation: Mixed-matrix membranes (MMMs) incorporating porous fillers like MOFs or carbon nanotubes offer a promising route to enhance gas separation performance. However, the precise relationship between filler properties (loading, size, surface chemistry) and the resulting polymer chain packing and free volume distribution remains poorly understood, leading to unpredictable performance and suboptimal filler selection. A systematic, quantitative study is needed to move beyond empirical design and enable the rational creation of next-generation membranes with tailored transport properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nanofiller loading, size, and surface chemistry dictate polymer chain packing and free volume through specific interfacial interactions and confinement effects, and that an optimal combination exists to maximize selective gas transport by simultaneously enhancing diffusivity selectivity and sorption capacity.
proposals.method: We will fabricate MMMs using a model polymer (e.g., polyimide Matrimid) and systematically vary the loading (5-30 wt%), size (1-100 nm), and surface chemistry (hydrophilic, hydrophobic, functionalized) of MOF fillers (e.g., ZIF-8, UiO-66). Free volume and chain packing will be characterized using Positron Annihilation Lifetime Spectroscopy (PALS) and Wide-Angle X-ray Scattering (WAXS). Gas transport properties (permeability, selectivity for CO2/CH4 and O2/N2 pairs) will be measured, and the data will be analyzed using Maxwell and modified Nielsen models to correlate filler parameters with observed changes in free volume and performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish quantitative structure-property relationships linking nanofiller characteristics to polymer microstructure in MMMs. It will provide a predictive framework for selecting and tailoring fillers to achieve specific free volume architectures, directly advancing the rational design of high-performance separation membranes. The findings will also offer fundamental insights into polymer-nanoparticle interfacial phenomena relevant to nanocomposite science.
proposals.novelty: While filler effects on permeability are known, this study uniquely combines PALS and WAXS to directly quantify how filler parameters *precisely* manipulate nanoscale polymer chain packing and free volume elements, moving beyond correlative performance studies to mechanistic, microstructural control.

proposals.title: Subnanoporous MOF-Polymer Nanocomposite Membranes for Selective Organic Liquid Separations
proposals.motivation: Separating organic liquid mixtures, such as aliphatic/aromatic isomers or aqueous/organic solutions, is critical for chemical and pharmaceutical manufacturing but remains energy-intensive. Traditional distillation dominates, and while polymer membranes exist for some organics, they often lack the precise, sub-nanometer selectivity needed for molecules of similar size and polarity. The Freeman group's recent work on constructing angstrom-scale ion channels using metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) for ionic selectivity presents a promising but unexplored platform for liquid-phase molecular separations.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating MOFs with tailored pore apertures and chemistry into polymer matrices will create nanocomposite membranes whose subnanoscale channels can discriminate between organic molecules based on subtle differences in size, shape, and polarity in the liquid phase.
proposals.method: We will synthesize nanocomposite membranes by incorporating pre-2026 characterized MOFs (e.g., ZIF-8 for ~3.4 Å pores, UiO-66 derivatives) into high-performance polymers like polyimide or PIM-1. Membranes will be fabricated as dense films via solution casting. Separation performance will be evaluated using custom pervaporation and organic solvent nanofiltration rigs for model mixtures (e.g., xylene isomers, methanol/MTBE). Sorption and diffusion properties will be decoupled using pre-2026 gravimetric vapor sorption for pure components and spectroscopic analysis of sorbed liquid mixtures to understand partitioning effects, extending methods from recent ion pairing studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-property relationships linking MOF pore geometry and surface functionality to organic liquid mixture selectivity in nanocomposite membranes. It will expand the application of subnanochannel design principles from ion transport to molecular separations, providing a new materials platform for energy-efficient liquid separations.
proposals.novelty: While MOF-polymer composites have been studied for gas separation, their deliberate application to organic liquid separations using precise, sub-nanometer channels is novel. This extends the group's core expertise in ionic subnanochannels and ion pairing effects into the underexplored realm of discriminating neutral organic solutes.

proposals.title: Predictive Modeling of Gas Transport in MOF-Polymer Nanocomposite Membranes: Linking Free Volume Architecture to Permeability and Selectivity
proposals.motivation: The incorporation of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) into polymer membranes significantly enhances gas separation performance, but the underlying mechanisms are poorly quantified. Pre-2026, predictive models largely treat nanocomposites as homogeneous media, failing to capture how nanofiller geometry and dispersion alter the polymer's free volume distribution, which is the critical determinant of sorption and transport properties. A quantitative, structure-property model is needed to rationally design next-generation membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: The permeability and selectivity of a MOF-polymer nanocomposite membrane can be predicted by a computational model that quantifies the nanofiller-induced redistribution of free volume elements (FVEs) and their connectivity, which dictates the transport pathways for gas molecules.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale computational framework. First, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations will be used to generate atomistic models of nanocomposites with varied MOF types (e.g., ZIF-8, UiO-66) and loadings in a model polymer (e.g., polyimide). The Connolly surface method will be applied to trajectory data to map the size, shape, and spatial distribution of FVEs. A graph theory-based network analysis will then be implemented to quantify FVE connectivity and identify percolating pathways. Finally, a kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) algorithm will simulate gas molecule hopping between FVEs, with hopping rates derived from transition state theory using energy barriers calculated from MD, to predict permeability and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, mechanistic link between nanofiller characteristics and the emergent free volume architecture in nanocomposite membranes. It will deliver a predictive tool for screening MOF-polymer pairs for specific gas separations (e.g., CO2/CH4, O2/N2). The model will advance the field from empirical design to a fundamentally guided paradigm for engineering membrane nanostructure.
proposals.novelty: While free volume is known to be important, this proposal uniquely integrates graph theory and kinetic Monte Carlo to model transport explicitly through the *network* of interconnected, filler-modified free volume elements, moving beyond average property descriptors like fractional free volume.

proposals.title: Long-Term Stability of Subnanometer Ion-Selective Membranes: Assessing Plasticization Resistance Under Continuous Organic Vapor Exposure
proposals.motivation: The development of subnanometer channels, such as those from MOFs or artificial constructs, has revolutionized selective ion transport for separations and energy applications. However, their long-term stability under realistic process conditions, especially in the presence of aggressive organic compounds that can swell or plasticize the polymer matrix, remains largely uncharacterized. This gap hinders the translation of these advanced materials from lab-scale demonstrations to industrial deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the chemical architecture and rigidity of the polymer matrix surrounding subnanometer channels will dictate the membrane's resistance to plasticization and performance decay under continuous exposure to organic vapors, with cross-linked or glassy polymers offering superior long-term stability compared to rubbery polymers.
proposals.method: We will fabricate thin-film composite membranes featuring state-of-the-art subnanometer channels (e.g., MOF-based or artificial sodium-selective channels) embedded in polymer matrices with varying rigidity (e.g., cross-linked polyamide, glassy polyimide, rubbery polydimethylsiloxane). Membranes will be subjected to continuous cross-flow filtration of saline feeds containing trace aggressive organics (e.g., toluene, acetone vapors). Performance (salt rejection, water/solvent flux, ion selectivity) will be monitored over >1000 hours. Sorption studies, similar to prior ion-pairing work, will quantify organic uptake, while techniques like dynamic mechanical analysis will track changes in polymer relaxation behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish critical structure-property relationships linking polymer matrix chemistry to the operational longevity of subnanometer-channel membranes. It will provide a validated testing protocol for assessing plasticization resistance and yield design principles for creating membranes that maintain high selectivity under chemically challenging conditions.
proposals.novelty: While subnanometer channel fabrication is an active area, systematic studies of their long-term stability and failure modes under organic exposure are lacking. This proposal uniquely combines Freeman group expertise in sorption, polymer properties, and transport to address this durability gap for next-generation membranes.

proposals.title: Scalable Fabrication of Ultrathin, Defect-Free Discrete Nanofluidic Heterostructure (DNH) Membranes for Enhanced Water Permeance in Desalination
proposals.motivation: The desalination field urgently needs membranes that combine high ion selectivity with exceptional water flux to reduce energy consumption. Discrete Nanofluidic Heterostructures (DNHs), which incorporate sub-nanometer channels like those in MOFs or artificial constructs, offer precise molecular sieving but are notoriously difficult to fabricate as large-area, defect-free, and ultrathin films. Current methods often yield thick, unsupported, or defective layers that limit practical water permeance and scalability for pressure-driven processes like reverse osmosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by employing a sequential, layer-by-layer (LbL) interfacial assembly technique on a tailored porous support, we can fabricate ultrathin (<100 nm), continuous, and defect-free DNH membranes that exhibit order-of-magnitude increases in water permeance while maintaining high salt rejection.
proposals.method: We will fabricate DNH membranes using a cationic polyelectrolyte and anionic MOF nanoparticles (e.g., ZIF-8 with ~3.4 Å pores) via automated, precise LbL dip-coating onto a highly smooth, ultraporous polysulfone support. The assembly conditions (pH, ionic strength, dipping time) will be optimized to promote dense, interlocked packing of the MOF particles, forming a continuous selective layer. Membrane performance will be evaluated in a cross-flow RO system using NaCl and MgSO4 solutions, with permeance and rejection measured. Advanced characterization (SEM, TEM, XPS) will correlate layer structure and continuity with transport properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable processing route for defect-free, ultrathin DNH membranes, directly translating the promising transport properties of discrete nanochannels into a practical membrane architecture. It will provide fundamental insights into the relationship between interfacial assembly parameters, film morphology, and desalination performance, guiding future design of high-flux selective layers.
proposals.novelty: While DNH concepts exist, the application of a controlled LbL interfacial assembly specifically to create an ultrathin, continuous selective layer from discrete nanofluidic particles on a robust support for high-pressure desalination is novel and addresses the critical fabrication gap between nanomaterial discovery and membrane module development.

proposals.title: Design of Sub-Nanometer Polymer/MOF Hybrid Membranes for Selective Transport in Multi-Component Acid Gas Streams
proposals.motivation: Industrial gas separations, such as natural gas sweetening or hydrogen purification, involve complex streams containing CO2, H2S, water vapor, and hydrocarbons. Traditional membrane design, optimized for binary gas pairs like CO2/CH4, fails under these conditions due to competitive sorption, plasticization, and complex transport coupling. A predictive framework for membrane performance in multi-component mixtures is critically needed to enable energy-efficient, real-world separations.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with precisely tuned sub-nanometer pores into glassy polymer matrices, we can create hybrid membranes where the MOF pores act as selective, size-sieving channels for target gases (e.g., CO2), while the polymer matrix controls the overall mechanical integrity and mitigates the deleterious effects of strongly-sorbing impurities like H2S and H2O on membrane stability.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of hybrid membranes by incorporating pre-synthesized, angstrom-scale MOF crystals (e.g., ZIF-8, MIL-53 variants) into high-performance polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs). Membrane characterization will include sorption measurements using a high-pressure gravimetric analyzer for multi-component gas mixtures (CO2, CH4, H2S, H2O) to quantify competitive sorption and plasticization. Gas transport properties (permeability, selectivity) will be measured for pure gases and ternary/quaternary mixtures. Data will be analyzed using Maxwell-based models for composite transport and the Dual-Mode Sorption model extended for mixed gases to deconvolute sorption and diffusion effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental structure-property relationship for hybrid membranes under industrially relevant multi-component feed conditions. It will provide quantitative data and models on how impurities alter the sorption and transport of primary gas pairs, moving the field beyond binary gas testing. The project will deliver guidelines for designing MOF-polymer interfaces to resist impurity-induced performance decay.
proposals.novelty: While MOF-polymer hybrids and sub-nanochannels have been studied for binary separations, their systematic evaluation and design for complex, impurity-laden streams is unexplored. The novelty lies in explicitly using MOF pores as designed, stable transport pathways to circumvent the plasticization and competitive sorption that plague pure polymer membranes in such environments.

proposals.title: Cascaded Membrane Systems with MOF-Based Subnanochannels for Ultra-High Purity Ion Separations
proposals.motivation: Achieving ultra-high purity in critical separations, such as lithium extraction or pharmaceutical salt purification, is hindered by a fundamental trade-off between membrane selectivity and permeability. Highly selective materials, like sub-nanometer channels, often have low throughput and are susceptible to fouling and degradation in complex feeds. A system-level design that decouples bulk processing from final purification is needed to make such selective materials industrially viable.
proposals.hypothesis: A cascaded hybrid separation system, where a high-flux, fouling-resistant bulk separation stage protects and concentrates the feed for a final ultra-selective stage, will achieve target ultra-high purities (>99.9%) at significantly higher overall throughput and lower energy cost than a single-stage process using the selective material alone.
proposals.method: We will design and test a two-stage cascade. Stage 1 will utilize robust, high-permeability polymer membranes (e.g., from polyamide or sulfonated polymers) for bulk desalination and concentration. Stage 2 will employ ultra-selective membranes featuring precisely engineered sub-nanometer channels, building on recent work with artificial sodium-selective channels and metal-organic framework (MOF) based angstrom-scale pores. The inter-stage stream chemistry will be optimized using pre-2026 models for ion pairing and sorption (e.g., from sulfate salt studies in cation exchange membranes) to precondition the feed for the final stage. System performance will be evaluated for model separations like Na+/Li+ or Cl-/SO4^2-.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated framework for designing cascaded membrane systems that break the selectivity-permeability trade-off at the system level. It will yield new insights into the coupling between bulk membrane transport properties and the performance requirements for ultimate purification stages, guiding the integration of emerging selective materials into practical processes.
proposals.novelty: While cascades are known in distillation, their strategic application to protect and enable the use of fragile, ultra-selective synthetic subnanochannels in membrane separations is novel. The approach explicitly uses the bulk stage to manage fouling and ion-pairing effects, which are typically detrimental to high-selectivity materials, creating a synergistic system architecture.

proposals.title: In-Situ Fabrication of Sub-Nanometer Ion-Selective Membranes within Reactor and Pipeline Walls for Integrated Separation
proposals.motivation: Conventional membrane systems are modular add-ons, requiring separate housings, connections, and support structures, which increase system footprint, capital cost, and energy losses. Integrating separation directly into process infrastructure could revolutionize chemical manufacturing and water treatment by enabling more compact, efficient, and intrinsically safer processes. This addresses a critical need in process intensification, a major pre-2026 industrial goal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that thin-film, ion-selective polymer membranes can be fabricated in-situ on the interior surfaces of metallic or ceramic reactors and pipelines, creating a continuous, integrated separation layer that maintains high permselectivity and stability under process conditions.
proposals.method: We will develop a sequential, in-situ interfacial polymerization technique to form polyamide or polyelectrolyte multilayer films directly on the inner walls of tubular reactors. Using pre-2026 knowledge of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and sub-nanochannels, we will incorporate tailored MOF nanoparticles (e.g., ZIF-8 for size exclusion) or graphene oxide nanosheets into the polymer matrix during formation to create angstrom-scale ion transport pathways. The transport properties and salt sorption behavior of these integrated membranes will be characterized using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and radiotracer methods, similar to recent work on ion pairing in cation exchange membranes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational fabrication methodology for structurally integrated membrane systems, moving beyond the paradigm of standalone modules. It will provide new data on the sorption and transport of salts in constrained, composite films formed on non-ideal substrates, contributing to the fundamental understanding of membranes in non-standard geometries.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving membrane fabrication from a dedicated manufacturing step to an in-situ, on-demand process within the final application infrastructure, creating a seamless, monolithic separation unit rather than an inserted component. This conceptual shift challenges the membrane system as a standalone unit.

proposals.title: Bio-Inspired Rigid Backbone Polymers for Frustrated Chain Packing: A Pathway to High-Performance CO2/CH4 Separation Membranes
proposals.motivation: The development of energy-efficient membranes for natural gas sweetening (CO2/CH4 separation) is critical for reducing processing costs and environmental impact. State-of-the-art glassy polymer membranes, like polyimides, face a well-known permeability/selectivity trade-off. Enhancing permeability often requires disrupting chain packing, but conventional methods can compromise mechanical stability and selectivity control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating bio-inspired, intrinsically rigid and contorted molecular motifs (e.g., triptycene, spirobifluorene) into glassy polymer backbones will create a network of interconnected, sub-nanometer cavities via frustrated chain packing, leading to simultaneous enhancements in CO2 permeability and CO2/CH4 selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of novel polyimides and polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) incorporating triptycene, spiro-centers, and other rigid, non-planar units. Polymer films will be characterized using positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) and wide-angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) to quantify cavity size and distribution. Pure-gas sorption (CO2, CH4) will be measured via gravimetric methods, and gas permeability/selectivity will be determined using constant-volume/variable-pressure apparatus. Structure-property relationships will be established by correlating backbone rigidity and contortion with sorption capacity, diffusivity selectivity, and overall separation performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for glassy polymer membranes by directly translating concepts from rigid protein scaffolds to synthetic macromolecular engineering. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how to precisely engineer interconnected microcavities for gas transport, moving beyond empirical approaches to packing disruption. The resulting materials aim to surpass the 2008 Robeson upper bound for CO2/CH4 separation.
proposals.novelty: While contorted molecules are used in PIMs, the systematic design of polymers using bio-inspired, rigid scaffolds specifically to create a *stable network* of interconnected cavities for selective transport is novel. This approach merges concepts from synthetic ion channel design with advanced membrane materials science.

proposals.title: A Biomimetic, Facilitated Transport Membrane-Solvent Hybrid System for Enhanced CO2 Capture: Integrating Sub-Nanoscale Channels with Reactive Amino Acid Salt Carriers
proposals.motivation: Conventional CO2 capture technologies, such as amine scrubbing and polymeric membrane separation, face significant trade-offs between energy efficiency, selectivity, and capacity. The urgent need for more efficient carbon capture processes, driven by climate change mitigation goals, requires innovative approaches that transcend these limitations by synergizing the strengths of existing methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coupling a thin, highly selective membrane containing sub-nanoscale channels with a flowing solvent containing a non-volatile, reactive CO2 carrier (e.g., an amino acid salt) will create a facilitated transport mechanism that significantly enhances CO2 flux and selectivity over N2 and O2, overcoming the permeability-selectivity trade-off of standalone membranes.
proposals.method: We will fabricate thin-film composite membranes featuring sub-nanoscale ion channels, inspired by recent work on metal-organic framework and artificial subnanochannel membranes, to provide precise size-based selectivity. This membrane will be interfaced with a flowing aqueous solvent containing potassium sarcosinate as a model, non-volatile reactive carrier. Sorption and transport experiments will measure CO2, N2, and O2 permeability and selectivity under varied conditions. The methodology will explicitly account for ion pairing effects on salt sorption within the membrane phase, adapting techniques from prior work on cation exchange membranes, to understand and optimize the interfacial coupling between the membrane and the reactive solvent.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational understanding of mass transport in a novel hybrid membrane-absorption architecture. It will provide quantitative design principles for coupling selective membranes with reactive solvents, contributing new knowledge to the fields of membrane science and gas separation. The results could define a new pathway for high-performance, energy-efficient carbon capture systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the biomimetic system integration of a size-selective, sub-nanoscale channel membrane with a flowing chemical facilitator, creating an engineered analogue to alveolar gas exchange. This specific combination of a tailored solid-state membrane with a non-volatile liquid-phase carrier for CO2 capture is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Discovery of Subnanochannel-Forming Polymers for CO2/N2 Separation via Automated Inkjet Printing and Parallelized Permeation Testing
proposals.motivation: Efficient carbon capture from flue gas requires polymer membranes with high CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity, but the traditional Edisonian approach to material discovery is prohibitively slow. The Robeson upper bound for this gas pair remains a significant challenge, and new materials are needed to surpass it for industrial deployment. Accelerating the exploration of polymer chemical space, particularly for materials that can form tailored, angstrom-scale transport channels, is a critical bottleneck in membrane development.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting high-throughput experimentation (HTE) platforms from heterogeneous catalysis to combinatorially synthesize and screen polymer libraries, we can rapidly identify novel polymer formulations that create sub-nanometer selective channels, leading to exceptional CO2/N2 separation performance that exceeds the current upper bound.
proposals.method: We will employ an automated inkjet printing system to deposit combinatorial arrays of polymer precursor 'inks'—systematically varying monomers (e.g., PEG-based, TR polymers), crosslinkers, and functional additives (e.g., MOF nanoparticles for channel definition)—onto porous supports. After curing, a custom-built, multiplexed time-lag apparatus with a multi-stream gas feed and rapid-response mass spectrometry detection will measure pure-gas CO2 and N2 permeability for each library member in parallel. Data analysis will correlate composition, inferred channel properties from sorption isotherms, and separation performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new HTE pipeline for polymer membrane discovery, generating an unprecedented dataset linking polymer chemistry to gas transport. It will yield specific, high-performance candidate materials for carbon capture and provide fundamental insights into the design rules for creating polymer subnanochannels that selectively transport CO2.
proposals.novelty: While HTE is mature in catalysis, its application to fabricate and test functional, defect-free polymer membranes in a combinatorial format is novel. The direct integration of channel-forming concepts (e.g., from MOF or subnanochannel research) into a rapid, automated synthesis-and-screen cycle for polymers represents a significant methodological leap.

proposals.title: Mapping the Viscoelastic-Performance Landscape: A High-Throughput Platform for Discovery of Polymers for CO2/N2 Separation
proposals.motivation: Polymer membranes for post-combustion carbon capture are constrained by the traditional trade-off between permeability and selectivity, often framed by the Robeson upper bound. This trade-off is intrinsically linked to polymer dynamics, with a sharp dichotomy between rubbery and glassy states. However, the vast, continuous design space of polymers with intermediate viscoelastic properties remains largely unexplored for gas separation, limiting the discovery of materials that could transcend conventional performance limits.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically tuning a polymer's viscoelastic spectrum—through controlled variations in backbone flexibility, side-chain mobility, and crosslink density—will reveal non-monotonic relationships with CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity, leading to the identification of novel formulations with performance maxima in traditionally underexplored regions of the dynamic mechanical landscape.
proposals.method: We will adapt combinatorial synthesis and rapid testing methods from pharmaceutical discovery. A high-throughput robotic platform will synthesize libraries of acrylate- and imide-based copolymers and hybrids, varying monomer ratios, chain length, and incorporation of dynamic crosslinkers (e.g., Diels-Alder adducts). Each polymer will be characterized via high-throughput dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) and broadband dielectric spectroscopy to map viscoelastic properties (e.g., tan δ, relaxation times). Parallel, automated time-lag apparatuses and mixed-gas permeation cells will measure CO2 and N2 permeability and selectivity at 35°C and relevant feed pressures. Data will be analyzed using machine learning (e.g., Gaussian Process Regression) to model and predict performance from viscoelastic descriptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first extensive, quantitative dataset linking fundamental polymer dynamics to gas transport performance, moving beyond binary classifications. It will produce a predictive framework for designing membranes based on targeted viscoelastic properties and is expected to identify new high-performing polymer families that occupy previously uncharted regions on the Robeson plot for CO2/N2 separation.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its deliberate, high-throughput creation and screening of a continuous spectrum of viscoelastic states, rather than comparing pre-existing glassy and rubbery polymers. It directly integrates dynamic mechanical property measurement with gas transport testing in a data-intensive campaign to uncover non-intuitive structure-property relationships.

proposals.title: CO2-Responsive Gating of Ion and Gas Transport in Membranes via pH-Triggered Redox Switching of Polyaniline-Based Polymers
proposals.motivation: Membranes that dynamically modulate permeability in response to environmental stimuli are critical for adaptive carbon capture, smart packaging, and controlled release. Current stimuli-responsive membranes often rely on electrical or thermal triggers, which are energy-intensive. Utilizing ambient CO2 as a chemical trigger offers a passive, energy-efficient pathway for autonomous control, directly addressing the need for responsive materials in carbon-concentrating environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating CO2-responsive, redox-active polymers (e.g., polyaniline) into membrane matrices will enable reversible, pH-triggered changes in polymer chain conformation and cross-link density, thereby creating a membrane whose ionic and gas transport properties can be switched by exposure to CO2 or its removal.
proposals.method: We will synthesize membranes by blending or grafting polyaniline (PANI), whose conductivity and hydrophilicity are pH-dependent, into polymer matrices like polyelectrolyte multilayers or cross-linked hydrogels. Membrane transport properties (salt permeability, gas permeance) will be characterized using pre-2026 established techniques (e.g., diffusion cells, time-lag apparatus) under alternating atmospheres of CO2/N2. The role of ion pairing and water sorption, informed by Freeman's prior work on ion exchange membranes, will be analyzed to decouple swelling effects from specific gating mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design principle for chemically gated, CO2-responsive membranes, expanding the toolbox beyond electrochromic analogs. It will provide quantitative structure-property relationships linking polymer redox state, membrane morphology, and switchable transport, contributing new knowledge to the fields of stimuli-responsive polymers and membrane science.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly translating the electrical gating mechanism of electrochromic materials to a purely chemical CO2/pH trigger for membrane permeability control, and in systematically applying established sorption and transport models to this new, autonomously switching system.

proposals.title: Bioinspired Topological Optimization of Asymmetric Membrane Pore Networks Using Vascular Growth Algorithms
proposals.motivation: Asymmetric membranes, critical for high-flux, selective separations like desalination and gas purification, face a fundamental trade-off between permeability and selectivity. The morphology of the porous support layer significantly influences hydraulic resistance and concentration polarization, yet its design remains largely empirical. A physics-based, systematic approach to optimize the 3D pore network architecture could break this trade-off, enhancing membrane performance for energy-intensive separations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying vascular network optimization principles, such as Murray's law for minimum work and shear-stress-driven adaptation, to computationally design the pore architecture of asymmetric membranes will yield structures that maximize permeability for a target solute at a given pressure drop while maintaining the selectivity of the skin layer.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational algorithm that treats the membrane's pore network as a growing vasculature. Starting from a dense, selective skin layer with defined pore size (e.g., sub-nanometer for ions), the algorithm will iteratively grow and prune branching pores into the macroporous support based on local shear stress and target solute flux, minimizing total energy dissipation. The model will incorporate pre-2026 knowledge of ion/solute transport and sorption (e.g., accounting for ion pairing effects) to define local transport resistances. The optimized 3D geometries will then be fabricated using emerging techniques like 3D printing of polymer templates or controlled phase inversion guided by the computational output.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physics-based design framework for next-generation asymmetric membranes, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will yield specific, testable membrane architectures predicted to exhibit superior flux-selectivity combinations. The resulting algorithm and design principles will be a new tool in membrane science for tailoring membranes to specific solutes, such as salts or gases.
proposals.novelty: While vascular optimization is established in physiology, its application to engineer the internal pore topology of synthetic separation membranes is novel. This approach fundamentally shifts membrane design from crafting homogeneous materials to computationally engineering the optimal 3D fluid pathway, a concept not explored in pre-2026 membrane science.

proposals.title: Dynamic Topology Optimization of Asymmetric Polymer Membranes for Adaptive Biogas Upgrading
proposals.motivation: Biogas upgrading via membrane separation is a key technology for renewable energy, but conventional membranes suffer from a fixed performance trade-off between CO2 permeability and CO2/CH4 selectivity, limiting efficiency under variable feed conditions. Real-world biogas streams exhibit fluctuations in composition and pressure, demanding membranes that can adapt to maintain optimal separation. Current membrane design lacks a systematic framework to engineer pore morphology across multiple scales for dynamic process optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating real-time process variables into a multi-scale topological optimization algorithm, we can generate blueprints for asymmetric membranes whose pore network architecture is specifically engineered to maximize system-level methane recovery and energy efficiency under variable operating conditions.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale computational model linking atomistic simulations of gas sorption and transport in sub-nanochannels (informed by Freeman's work on MOF-based channels and ion pairing) to continuum-scale performance. A density-based topological optimization algorithm, using adjoint methods for gradient computation, will iteratively design the 3D pore structure within a polymer matrix. The optimization objective function will dynamically incorporate real-time inputs (e.g., CO2 partial pressure) to adjust targets for local permeability and selectivity. The resulting blueprints will guide the fabrication of asymmetric membranes via phase inversion, with the selective layer morphology dictated by the optimized topology.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational-design-to-fabrication pipeline for adaptive membranes, moving beyond static materials to systems-aware separators. It will provide fundamental insights into how pore topology at the nano- and meso-scale governs dynamic separation performance. The outcome is a new paradigm for robust, energy-efficient biogas plants with higher methane recovery.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, dynamic feedback between real-time process conditions and the topological optimization of membrane pore architecture, a significant departure from designing for fixed, ideal gas mixtures. This fusion of real-time process control with multi-scale membrane topology design is unexplored.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Microfluidic Contactors Platform for Accelerating the Discovery of CO2-Resistant Protective Layers in H2/He-Selective Membranes
proposals.motivation: The purification of hydrogen and helium via membranes is critical for energy and industrial applications, but trace CO2 in feed streams can rapidly poison or plasticize the highly permeable polymer membranes required for efficient separation. Current material screening for protective layers is slow and fails to capture the complex interplay of competitive sorption and transport under realistic, mixed-gas conditions, hindering the development of robust composite membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a microfluidic membrane contactor array can rapidly quantify the CO2-selectivity and protective efficacy of novel sub-nanometer channel materials (e.g., tailored MOFs, polymers of intrinsic microporosity) by directly measuring their influence on the degradation kinetics of an underlying H2-selective polymer layer under mixed-gas feeds.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-based microfluidic chip with an array of parallel membrane contactor units. Each unit will integrate a thin-film composite membrane, where candidate protective layers (e.g., ZIF-8 variants, sulfonated polymers) are deposited atop a benchmark H2-selective polymer (e.g., a polyimide). Precise, computer-controlled gas mixing will deliver minute, defined H2/He/CO2 mixtures to each unit. Real-time, in-situ Raman spectroscopy and on-chip pressure/flow sensors will monitor CO2 sorption and the consequent changes in the underlying layer's permeability and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new high-throughput experimental paradigm for evaluating membrane degradation and protection. It will yield a quantitative dataset linking protective layer nanostructure (pore size, chemistry) to CO2 exclusion performance and H2 flux stability, providing design rules for next-generation composite membranes. The platform itself will be a valuable tool for accelerated materials discovery in gas separation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing microfluidics from a separation study tool into an accelerated, parallelized materials screening platform specifically for multi-layered membrane architectures, enabling direct, real-time observation of interfacial poisoning mechanisms that are difficult to capture with conventional testing.

proposals.title: Engineering Subnanometer Polymer Membranes for Passive, Controlled CO2 Delivery Using Osmotic Pressure Gradients
proposals.motivation: Precise CO2 delivery is critical for enhancing plant growth in greenhouses and biomass production in bioreactors, but conventional active injection systems are energy-intensive and can create fluctuating, non-uniform concentrations. A passive, membrane-based delivery system, analogous to transdermal drug patches, could provide a steady, low-energy flux of CO2, improving efficiency and control. This addresses a significant need in controlled environment agriculture and bioprocessing, where optimal and stable CO2 levels are key to productivity.
proposals.hypothesis: By tailoring the sorption and transport properties of polymer membranes to create specific, size-selective subnanometer channels, a stable osmotic pressure gradient can be harnessed to passively meter a precise and scalable flux of CO2 from a concentrated source into a target environment.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate thin-film composite membranes using polymers with high CO2 affinity (e.g., poly(ethylene oxide)-based block copolymers) and incorporate tailored additives or metal-organic framework (MOF) nanostructures to create defined transport pathways. Membrane properties—including thickness, crystallinity, and channel size—will be systematically varied. CO2 sorption will be measured via gravimetric analysis, and transport properties (permeability, selectivity over O2/N2) will be characterized using constant-pressure/variable-volume methods. The flux under controlled partial pressure gradients will be modeled and validated in a bench-scale simulated greenhouse/biorreactor system.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the fundamental design principles for using engineered osmotic membranes for passive gas delivery, a concept largely unexplored for CO2. It will provide new data on the relationship between polymer nanostructure, CO2 sorption (accounting for potential condensable gas effects), and controlled transmembrane flux under realistic gradients, advancing the field of membrane-based gas control beyond separation.
proposals.novelty: While membranes are extensively studied for CO2 separation from mixed gases, their application as a passive, rate-controlling delivery device for pure CO2, leveraging principles from transdermal drug delivery, is a novel inversion of the typical membrane function. The explicit focus on tailoring subnanometer channels for controlled delivery via a sustained osmotic gradient, rather than for high-flux separation, represents a distinct and non-obvious application of membrane transport science.

proposals.title: Subnanometer MOF-Polymer Composite Membranes for Ultra-Selective CO2 Delivery and Trace Contaminant Exclusion
proposals.motivation: The use of impure CO2 streams (e.g., flue gas, biogas) to feed high-value bioprocesses like pharmaceutical algae cultivation is limited by the risk of introducing trace contaminants (e.g., ethylene, VOCs) that can disrupt sensitive cultures. Current gas separation membranes often prioritize high CO2 permeability over the rigorous exclusion of these trace impurities, creating a critical purity bottleneck for advanced biomanufacturing and controlled agriculture.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with precisely tuned subnanometer pore apertures into a high-performance polymer matrix, we can fabricate composite membranes that achieve ultra-high CO2/contaminant selectivity via precise molecular sieving, while maintaining a moderate, controllable CO2 flux suitable for dosing applications.
proposals.method: We will synthesize MOF crystals (e.g., ZIF-8 variants, MIL-53) with pore dimensions tuned between the kinetic diameters of CO2 (~3.3 Å) and key contaminants like ethylene (~4.2 Å). These MOFs will be incorporated as fillers into high-CO2-affinity polymer matrices (e.g., polymers of intrinsic microporosity, PIMs). Membrane performance will be characterized using mixed-gas permeation tests (CO2/N2/O2 with ppm-level ethylene or SO2) to measure selectivity and flux. Sorption studies will quantify contaminant uptake, and transport models will account for ion pairing and competitive sorption effects on permeability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for CO2 delivery membranes that prioritizes contaminant exclusion over raw flux. It will provide fundamental structure-property relationships for subnanometer sieving in mixed-gas environments and deliver a prototype membrane material enabling the safe use of impure CO2 sources in purity-critical bioprocesses.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the typical membrane design goal, targeting moderate CO2 flux with extreme selectivity against specific trace contaminants, rather than maximizing CO2 permeability. It uniquely combines pre-2026 advances in subnanometer MOF pore engineering with Freeman group expertise in sorption and transport in polymers to solve a previously unaddressed 'inverse' purification problem for bioprocess feeds.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Membrane Architecture for Helium Purification: Mitigating CO2-Induced Plasticization in Polymeric Membranes with a Metal-Organic Framework-Based Pre-Separation Layer
proposals.motivation: Helium recovery from natural gas is critical for medical and high-tech industries, but feed streams often contain CO2, which can plasticize and poison conventional glassy polymer membranes used for He/CH4 separation, drastically reducing selectivity and lifetime. Pre-2026 membrane research has focused on either developing highly CO2-selective materials or robust He-selective materials, but not on integrating them to solve this specific poisoning problem in a single, engineered unit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a thin, defect-free layer of a CO2-selective metal-organic framework (MOF) can be integrated as a protective pre-layer on a high-performance He-selective polymer membrane, selectively removing CO2 upstream and thereby preventing plasticization of the underlying polymer, leading to stable, high-purity helium permeation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize sub-nanometer CO2-selective channels using a well-defined MOF (e.g., ZIF-8 or a tailored Mg-MOF-74 analog) to create a thin, selective layer on a porous support. This MOF layer will be characterized for its CO2/CH4 and CO2/He separation properties. It will then be integrated via sequential fabrication onto a benchmark He-selective polymer membrane (e.g., a thermally rearranged polymer or a polymer of intrinsic microporosity). The hybrid membrane's performance (He/CH4 selectivity, He permeability) and stability will be tested under simulated natural gas feed containing aggressive concentrations of CO2 (5-20 mol%), with performance compared to the unprotected polymer membrane.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel membrane design principle for treating multicomponent feeds by decoupling selectivity requirements into separate, optimized layers. It will yield a fundamental understanding of how a protective pre-layer mitigates condensable gas poisoning, extending the operational envelope of high-performance polymeric membranes. The proposed architecture could significantly advance the practical viability of membrane-based helium extraction.
proposals.novelty: While bilayer membranes exist, the specific use of a CO2-selective MOF layer not for final separation but explicitly as a protective 'sacrificial' barrier to prevent plasticization of a downstream He-selective polymer membrane is a novel, functionally graded approach to membrane poisoning. It leverages pre-2026 advances in angstrom-scale MOF channels and polymer membrane science in a new, synergistic configuration.

proposals.title: A Non-Equilibrium Thermodynamic Framework for Coupled Sorption-Diffusion in Sub-Nanometer MOF and Polymer Membranes
proposals.motivation: Current models for ion and gas transport in nanoporous membranes, such as the solution-diffusion model, treat sorption equilibrium and Fickian diffusion as independent, sequential processes. This fails to capture dynamic coupling, where strong binding can retard mobility or cooperative sorption can enhance it, leading to inaccurate predictions of selectivity and flux, especially in sub-nanometer channels like those in MOFs or selective polymers. A rigorous framework is needed to model these coupled phenomena, analogous to how non-equilibrium thermodynamics resolved coupled selectivity-permeability in biological ion channels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the formalism of non-equilibrium thermodynamics (NET) from biophysics—specifically using coupled flux equations with Onsager cross-coefficients—will quantitatively describe how sorption affinity dynamically influences diffusional mobility in synthetic nanoporous materials, leading to more accurate predictions of transport under mixed-gas or mixed-salt conditions.
proposals.method: We will develop a NET model where fluxes of species (e.g., ions, gases) are linear functions of chemical potential gradients, with phenomenological coefficients (L_ij) linking all fluxes. These coefficients will be determined experimentally for model systems (e.g., sodium-selective MOF channels, cation exchange membranes) using time-dependent sorption and permeation data. The model will be implemented computationally to solve the coupled differential equations, and validated against transport data for competitive sorption scenarios (e.g., CO2/CH4 mixtures, Na+/K+ salts) where standard models deviate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical and computational tool for predicting coupled transport in next-generation membranes, moving beyond the sequential solution-diffusion paradigm. It will directly explain anomalous selectivity and flux data in sub-nanochannels and guide the design of materials where sorption and diffusion are intentionally coupled for enhanced separation performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of a biophysical NET framework, used for ion channels, to synthetic porous materials and membranes, explicitly coupling sorption and diffusion through cross-coefficients—a departure from the standard independent treatment in materials science.

proposals.title: Combinatorial Synthesis and In-Situ Characterization of Covalently Graded Interfaces for Robust, High-Performance Composite Membranes
proposals.motivation: Composite membranes, which combine disparate materials like polymers and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), offer unparalleled potential for separations but are plagued by interfacial delamination and failure under operational stress. This interfacial weakness remains a critical bottleneck for durability in demanding applications such as desalination and gas separation. A systematic understanding of how to design and synthesize mechanically robust, chemically stable interfaces is lacking, limiting the translation of advanced materials into practical membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that creating a covalently bonded, compositionally graded interfacial region between polymer and filler phases (e.g., MOFs) will eliminate sharp property discontinuities, thereby dramatically enhancing mechanical integrity and long-term performance without sacrificing the intrinsic transport selectivity of the constituent materials.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput combinatorial synthesis platform to fabricate membrane libraries. This platform will systematically vary interfacial parameters such as crosslinker density, gradient length, and chemistry of coupling agents (e.g., silanes, acrylates) between a polyamide matrix and MOF fillers. In-situ characterization will integrate Raman spectroscopy and quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation (QCM-D) monitoring during interfacial synthesis to track chemical bonding and viscoelastic evolution in real-time. Post-synthesis, membranes will be evaluated for salt rejection, water flux, and subjected to mechanical peel tests and pressure-cycling durability assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish quantitative structure-property relationships linking specific interfacial architectures to composite membrane durability and performance. It will yield a validated set of design principles for engineering robust interfaces, moving beyond trial-and-error. The developed high-throughput platform will serve as a new paradigm for accelerating the discovery of next-generation heterogeneous membranes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated combinatorial and in-situ approach, which treats the interface as a primary, tunable design variable rather than a passive junction. This shifts the focus from merely mitigating interface failure to actively engineering it as a performance-enhancing functional zone, a concept not fully explored in pre-2026 membrane science.

proposals.title: Decoupling Sorption and Diffusion Contributions to Water/Salt Selectivity in Subnanometer Polymer and MOF-Based Membranes
proposals.motivation: The overall permeability selectivity of a membrane for water over salt is a product of sorption selectivity and diffusion selectivity. However, these contributions are typically conflated in standard permeability measurements, hindering rational membrane design. A quantitative framework to decouple these factors is critically needed to guide the development of next-generation desalination and ion-separation membranes, especially for emerging subnanometer channels like those in MOFs and polymers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for rigid, size-sieving subnanometer channels (e.g., MOF pores), diffusion selectivity will dominate overall water/salt selectivity, whereas for flexible polymer membranes, sorption selectivity, influenced by ion pairing and polymer chemistry, will be a significant or controlling factor.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled experimental-theoretical approach. Experimentally, we will measure water and salt (NaCl, Na2SO4) sorption isotherms via quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation monitoring (QCM-D) and vapor sorption analyzers for model polymer (e.g., sulfonated polymers) and MOF-based membranes. These sorption data will be combined with independently measured permeability coefficients from cross-flow filtration to back-calculate intrinsic diffusivities via the solution-diffusion model. Molecular dynamics simulations will be used to validate the decoupled transport parameters and probe the molecular basis of sorption and diffusion in the different pore environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative dataset explicitly separating sorption and diffusion selectivity for water/salt pairs in state-of-the-art subnanometer channels. It will yield a foundational design map, identifying which membrane property (sorption affinity or diffusional activation energy) to tune for a given material class to maximize selectivity.
proposals.novelty: While the solution-diffusion model is well-known, its rigorous application to decouple selectivity in ultra-confined, rigid MOF pores versus flexible polymer matrices, incorporating modern understanding of ion-pairing effects on sorption, is novel. This moves beyond reporting net performance to revealing the fundamental thermodynamic and kinetic levers controlling it.

proposals.title: Mapping the Diffusivity-Selectivity Tradeoff in Hydrated Polymer Membranes via an Adapted Percolation Theory Framework
proposals.motivation: A fundamental challenge in membrane science is the ubiquitous tradeoff between permeability and selectivity, often framed as an upper bound. While free volume and water content are recognized as key drivers of solute diffusivity, predictive models linking these structural parameters to transport performance remain qualitative. Drawing a quantitative analogy from the well-established percolation theory models for conductivity-strength tradeoffs in composite materials offers a powerful, unexplored framework to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the water-swollen polymer matrix can be modeled as a bicontinuous percolating network, where water clusters and polymer free volume elements act as the dual conductive phases, and that critical percolation thresholds for these phases dictate the observed upper bounds in diffusivity-selectivity plots.
proposals.method: We will adapt classical site and bond percolation models, and effective medium approximations, to treat water content (from gravimetric analysis) and polymer fractional free volume (from positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy) as the percolating components. The model will use water cluster connectivity and free volume element contiguity as fitting parameters, calibrated against extensive pre-2026 diffusivity data for ions (e.g., Na+, Cl-) and gases (e.g., CO2) in benchmark polymers like polyelectrolytes and polymers of intrinsic microporosity. Monte Carlo simulations will be employed to map structure-property relationships and identify percolation thresholds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, structure-based model predicting the diffusivity tradeoff from fundamental membrane hydration and free volume parameters. It will generate design maps identifying critical percolation thresholds to guide the synthesis of next-generation membranes, moving beyond empirical correlations. The adapted framework will establish a new theoretical lens for analyzing transport in complex, hydrated polymer systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual translation of percolation theory from composite conductivity-mechanical property tradeoffs to membrane diffusivity-selectivity tradeoffs, a formal analogy not previously established. This moves beyond statistical correlations to a physics-based model where water and free volume are explicitly treated as percolating networks.

proposals.title: Predictive Design of Ultrathin Ion-Selective Membranes via Layer-by-Layer Assembly and Free Volume Modeling
proposals.motivation: The design of advanced membranes for ion separations, such as lithium extraction or monovalent/divalent selectivity, is hindered by the empirical trade-off between permeability and selectivity. Pre-2026 work, including Freeman's on ion pairing in cation exchange membranes and sub-nanochannel fabrication, highlights the critical role of hydration and free volume. However, a predictive framework linking precise nanoscale architecture to transport properties is lacking, limiting de novo membrane design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ion transport properties of an ultrathin selective layer can be actively programmed by constructing a layer-by-layer (LbL) architecture with a controlled gradient in polymer chemistry, interlayer spacing, and hydration, and that this structure-property relationship can be captured in a predictive diffusivity-functionality map.
proposals.method: We will fabricate LbL membranes using alternating polyelectrolytes (e.g., PAH/PSS) and polyzwitterions, precisely controlling layer number, ionic strength, and pH to tune interlayer spacing (d-spacing) measured via ellipsometry and XRR. We will integrate these architectures with a modified free volume and hydration model, building on Freeman's prior work, to correlate d-spacing and water content with ion diffusivity. Machine learning (e.g., Gaussian Process Regression) will be used on the experimental dataset to generate predictive maps linking LbL sequence and processing to permeability-selectivity performance for target ions (e.g., Na+, Mg2+, SO42-).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind predictive design platform for LbL-based ion-selective membranes, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will deliver a validated set of 'transport maps' that directly link programmable nanoscale architecture to separation performance, providing a new paradigm for designing ultrathin composite membranes with tailored transport hierarchies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of the architectural precision of optical LbL assembly—traditionally used for coatings—with predictive transport modeling from membrane science to actively 'program' graded transport pathways, rather than merely optimizing a homogeneous film.

proposals.title: Quantifying Water State and Dynamics in Hydrated Ion-Exchange Membranes via Advanced NMR Spectroscopy: Linking Molecular Environment to Salt Sorption and Transport
proposals.motivation: In ion-exchange membranes for water purification and energy applications, water is not a passive solvent but a critical component governing ion sorption, pairing, and mobility. Pre-2026, the molecular-scale understanding of water state (bound vs. free) and dynamics within the sub-nanochannels of polymers and MOF-composite membranes remains limited, hindering the rational design of membranes with selective ion transport. Techniques like pulsed-field gradient NMR and relaxation measurements have been powerfully applied to probe water in fuel cell proton-exchange membranes but are underutilized for studying salt-sorbing systems central to Dr. Freeman's recent work on sulfate salts and artificial subnanochannels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the water mobility and hydrogen-bonding network within hydrated cation-exchange membranes and MOF-based subnanochannels, as quantified by NMR, directly dictate the extent of ion pairing and the effective diffusivity of specific salts, such as sodium sulfate. Specifically, membranes that promote more 'free-like' water will exhibit reduced ion pairing and enhanced anion transport.
proposals.method: We will employ a suite of solid-state and solution NMR techniques on hydrated membrane samples. 1H pulsed-field gradient NMR will measure water self-diffusion coefficients. 1H and 17O spin-lattice (T1) and spin-spin (T2) relaxation times will characterize water rotational dynamics and heterogeneity (bound/free populations). Two-dimensional 1H-1H exchange spectroscopy (EXSY) will probe water molecule exchange between different environments. These measurements will be performed on benchmark cation-exchange membranes (e.g., sulfonated polymers) and novel MOF-incorporated membranes from the group's prior work, under controlled hydration levels and in the presence of sorbed Na2SO4 and NaCl, enabling direct correlation with sorption and transport data from ion chromatography and conductivity measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish quantitative relationships between NMR-derived water state parameters and macroscopic salt sorption/transport properties, providing a missing molecular descriptor for membrane performance. It will yield a validated experimental framework to deconvolute the roles of polymer chemistry and nanochannel confinement on water structure, directly informing the design of next-generation selective membranes. The dataset will serve as a benchmark for computational studies of ion hydration in confined environments.
proposals.novelty: While NMR is standard for fuel cell membranes, its systematic application to correlate water dynamics with ion-pairing effects in salt-sorbing cation-exchange membranes is novel. The integration of these molecular probes with the group's established expertise in constructing tailored subnanochannels (e.g., MOFs) to test structure-property hypotheses represents a significant methodological advance beyond standard sorption-transport characterization.

proposals.title: Percolating Metal-Organic Framework Networks as the Primary Selective Phase in Mixed Matrix Membranes for Ion Separations
proposals.motivation: Mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) traditionally use a continuous polymer matrix to provide mechanical integrity, with dispersed filler particles enhancing selectivity or permeability. However, the transport properties remain dominated by the polymer, limiting the exploitation of advanced fillers like sub-nanometer MOF channels with precise ion selectivity. Inverting the conventional architecture to create a percolating network of selective fillers could unlock unprecedented separation performance, particularly for challenging ion pairs like Na+/K+ or monovalent/divalent salts.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing a continuous, percolating network of ion-selective metal-organic framework (MOF) crystallites, stabilized by a minimal, interpenetrating polymer phase, the membrane's transport properties will be governed primarily by the intrinsic selectivity and sorption characteristics of the MOF channels, rather than the polymer matrix.
proposals.method: We will synthesize anisotropic, plate-like MOF particles (e.g., from pre-2026 work on 2D MOFs like ZIF-L) to facilitate percolation at low loadings. A solution-based, controlled evaporation technique will be used to induce oriented assembly of MOF particles into a continuous network within a casting solution of a highly permeable, inert polymer (e.g., poly(1-trimethylsilyl-1-propyne) or a cross-linkable oligomer). Membrane formation will be followed by in-situ cross-linking or vapor-induced phase separation to lock the structure. Transport properties (salt permeability, ion selectivity) will be characterized using diffusion cells and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, correlated with microstructure via SEM and XRD.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for MMMs where the filler constitutes the primary selective pathway, directly translating the exquisite selectivity of synthetic sub-nanochannels into macroscopic membrane performance. It will provide fundamental insights into the critical percolation thresholds and interfacial design needed to create continuous filler networks, advancing materials for precise ion separations in water treatment and resource recovery.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the fundamental role of the polymer from the continuous selective phase to a mere structural stabilizer, a significant departure from all pre-2026 MMM literature.
