It strategically leverages pre-2026 advancements in MOF-based angstrom-scale ion channels but re-architects the composite membrane to maximize their impact.

proposals.title: Scalable Fabrication of Defect-Free Mixed Matrix Membranes via Interfacial Engineering and Polymer-Filler Compatibility Design
proposals.motivation: Mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) incorporating advanced fillers like metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) or subnanoscale channels offer transformative potential for ion-selective separations and desalination. However, translating lab-scale performance to commercial scale is hindered by pervasive defects, filler agglomeration, and poor interfacial adhesion during large-area casting, leading to inconsistent transport properties and mechanical failure. A robust, scalable fabrication methodology is the critical bottleneck preventing the deployment of next-generation MMMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically engineering the polymer-filler interface through covalent and non-covalent interactions and optimizing high-shear, continuous casting parameters, we can fabricate large-area, defect-free MMMs with uniform filler dispersion and enhanced mechanical integrity, thereby preserving the intrinsic transport properties of the embedded selective fillers.
proposals.method: The approach will first screen polymer-MOF compatibility using Hansen solubility parameters and surface energy measurements to predict dispersion. We will then functionalize MOF particles (e.g., UiO-66, ZIF-8) with tailored surface ligands to promote covalent grafting or strong hydrogen bonding with polymer matrices (e.g., sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone), polyamide). Fabrication will utilize a pilot-scale, slot-die casting line with controlled shear and temperature profiles to induce shear alignment and prevent agglomeration. Membrane quality will be assessed via SEM/EDS, X-ray microtomography for 3D defect analysis, and dynamic mechanical analysis, correlated with salt diffusion and ion selectivity measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable framework for scaling MMM fabrication, directly linking interfacial chemistry and processing dynamics to membrane microstructure and performance. It will yield a prototype, meter-scale MMM roll with documented transport properties, providing a crucial pathway from lab discovery to industrial application for selective ion separation and water purification.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on discovering new filler properties, this proposal uniquely integrates interfacial thermodynamics and continuous polymer processing engineering—fields well-developed for neat polymers but rarely applied to MMMs—to solve the scale-up challenge, moving beyond simple solvent casting demonstrations.

proposals.title: Systematic Engineering of the Polymer-Filler Interfacial Zone in Mixed Matrix Membranes for Defect-Free Ion Transport
proposals.motivation: Mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) incorporating nanofillers like metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) or other sub-nanoscale channels promise enhanced selectivity and permeability. However, non-ideal polymer-filler interfaces create defective voids or rigidified polymer regions, compromising membrane performance and reproducibility. A systematic, molecular-level understanding and control of this interfacial zone is critically needed to realize the full potential of MMMs for precise ion separations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing polymer side-chain chemistry and filler surface functionality to promote specific, non-covalent interactions, we can create a continuous, defect-free interfacial zone with tailored transport properties that synergistically combine the selectivity of the filler with the processability of the polymer.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of polyelectrolytes and neutral polymers with tailored side-chain chemistries (e.g., charged groups, hydrogen-bond donors/acceptors). These will be combined with MOF fillers (e.g., ZIF-8, UiO-66) whose surfaces are functionalized via pre- or post-synthetic modification. The interfacial structure will be probed using positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) for free volume, dynamic mechanical analysis for rigidity gradients, and advanced electron microscopy. Ion transport properties (salt permeability, selectivity) will be measured and correlated with interfacial characterization, guided by models accounting for ion pairing and interfacial resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish design rules for the polymer-filler interface, moving beyond trial-and-error MMM fabrication. It will deliver a novel framework for creating next-generation membranes with predictable and enhanced ion separation performance by explicitly engineering the interfacial transport pathway.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on filler pore design, this proposal uniquely targets the *interfacial zone itself* as a critical, tunable transport domain, integrating Freeman group expertise in ion sorption, polymer physics, and sub-nanochannel transport into a unified design strategy.

proposals.title: Integrated Subnanochannel Membranes in Microfluidic Reactors for Enhanced In-Situ Product Separation
proposals.motivation: Microfluidic reactors offer precise control over chemical synthesis but lack efficient, integrated separation of products or catalysts, limiting their efficiency and requiring downstream processing. Pre-2026 advances in constructing angstrom-scale ion channels, particularly from metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and polymers, have created membranes with exceptional size- and charge-based selectivity. Integrating these ultra-thin, selective membranes directly into microfluidic architectures could enable continuous, membrane-assisted synthesis in a single, miniaturized platform.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a synthesized, ultra-thin polymer or MOF-based membrane with subnanometer-scale, ion-selective channels directly into a microfluidic channel wall will enable continuous, selective removal of ionic products or catalysts during synthesis, thereby shifting reaction equilibria and improving yield and purity.
proposals.method: We will fabricate microfluidic chips with a central reaction channel flanked by two parallel purification channels, separated by integrated membrane walls. The membrane will be formed in-situ via interfacial polymerization or by depositing and aligning pre-synthesized MOF nanosheets to create a dense, sub-1-nm selective layer. Using pre-2026 methods from the researcher's recent work, we will characterize membrane pore configuration, salt (e.g., NaCl, Na₂SO₄) sorption, and ion transport properties. Reaction and separation performance will be evaluated using a model equilibrium-limited reaction (e.g., esterification) where selective removal of a co-product (e.g., water, an ionic species) is tracked via on-chip sensors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design and fabrication protocol for hybrid membrane-microreactor devices. It will provide quantitative data on how confined transport and sorption properties of subnanochannels influence reaction kinetics and equilibria at the microscale, advancing the field of integrated process intensification.
proposals.novelty: While microreactors and selective membranes exist separately, their monolithic integration, leveraging the latest pre-2026 angstrom-channel designs for precise molecular separation within the reactor geometry itself, is a novel approach to process intensification not yet fully realized.

proposals.title: Mapping the Upper-Bound Relationship Between Permeability and Ion Selectivity in Polymers with Engineered Sub-Nanometer Free Volume
proposals.motivation: Membrane-based separations for water, energy, and resource recovery are fundamentally limited by a trade-off between permeability and selectivity. While empirical upper bounds exist for gas pairs, a rigorous, predictive framework for ion transport in aqueous environments is lacking. Defining these ultimate performance limits for ions would provide critical targets for designing next-generation polymer membranes with tailored free-volume architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a universal upper-bound relationship exists between ion permeability and selectivity in polymers, governed by the interplay of free-volume element geometry, polymer-ion solvation energetics, and coupled ion-water transport. This bound can be described by a modified solution-diffusion model incorporating constrained local equilibrium.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of polymers with systematically varied, rigid sub-nanometer free-volume architectures using metal-organic framework (MOF)-inspired templating and polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs). Ion sorption (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Cl-, SO42-) will be quantified via radiotracer and spectroscopic techniques, accounting for ion pairing. Transport properties (permeability, diffusivity, conductivity) will be measured under electrochemical gradients. A computational workflow combining molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of ion/water partitioning and kinetics with a modified Maxwell-Stefan framework will be used to model the data and extract the key scaling parameters defining the bound.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, mechanistic upper-bound relationship for ion transport in polymers, moving beyond empirical correlations. It will establish design rules linking free-volume architecture (size, shape, chemistry) to ultimate performance, providing a critical roadmap for synthesizing polymers for high-flux, selective separations like desalination and ion-specific recovery.
proposals.novelty: While upper bounds are established for gases, this proposal uniquely focuses on the more complex aqueous ion transport, integrating recent advances in constructing angstrom-scale channels with a fundamental analysis of sorption and transport thermodynamics to predict a intrinsic performance limit.

proposals.title: Ultrathin, Defect-Free Polymer Membranes via Interfacial MOF Templating for Enhanced Nanofiltration Flux and Selectivity
proposals.motivation: Membrane-based separations are limited by the permeability-selectivity trade-off, where reducing thickness to increase flux often compromises mechanical integrity and selectivity due to defects. Pre-2026 work on sub-nanochannels and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) has demonstrated precise molecular sieving, but integrating these materials into large-area, ultrathin (<100 nm) selective layers remains a critical fabrication challenge for scalable nanofiltration and sensing applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sacrificial, two-dimensional MOF layer formed at a liquid-liquid interface can template the formation of a contiguous, ultrathin, and defect-free polyamide selective layer via interfacial polymerization, decoupling thickness reduction from defect formation to achieve simultaneously high flux and ion selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize 2D Zn-based MOF nanosheets (e.g., ZIF-L) and assemble them into a monolayer at an oil-water interface. Interfacial polymerization of m-phenylenediamine and trimesoyl chloride will be conducted directly on this MOF template. The MOF will be subsequently etched using a mild acidic solution, leaving behind a freestanding, sub-100 nm polyamide film. Film thickness and morphology will be characterized by ellipsometry and SEM. Transport properties (water flux, salt rejection) will be measured using cross-flow filtration with NaCl, Na2SO4, and MgSO4 solutions, with sorption analyzed via quartz crystal microbalance to account for ion-pairing effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel interfacial templating method for fabricating large-area, ultrathin polymer membranes, directly addressing the permeability-selectivity trade-off. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of a 2D sacrificial scaffold in controlling polyamide formation and defect density, advancing the field towards scalable high-performance nanofiltration membranes.
proposals.novelty: While MOFs have been used as fillers or channels, using them as a sacrificial 2D template to guide the formation of an ultrathin, dense polymer layer is a novel fabrication strategy. This approach uniquely combines Freeman group expertise in sorption, polymer membranes, and sub-nanochannels to decouple thickness from defect formation.

proposals.title: Scalable Fabrication of Metal-Organic Framework-Incorporated Polymers for High-Flux, High-Selectivity Hollow Fiber Membranes in CO2/CH4 Separation
proposals.motivation: Industrial gas separations, such as CO2 removal from natural gas, demand energy-efficient membrane processes. While lab-scale flat-sheet membranes incorporating sub-nanometer channels (e.g., from MOFs or tailored polymers) show exceptional selectivity, their translation to high-surface-area, mechanically robust hollow fiber modules—the industry-preferred configuration—remains a critical bottleneck. Scaling up these advanced materials without compromising their precise transport properties is a key challenge for deploying next-generation membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by optimizing polymer dope formulations and non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS) spinning parameters, we can successfully scale the fabrication of polymers containing tailored angstrom-scale channels into defect-free hollow fibers that retain the high gas selectivity and permeability observed in flat-sheet analogs.
proposals.method: We will synthesize polymers with tailored free volume elements, informed by prior work on ion pairing and sorption, and incorporate pre-synthesized MOF nanoparticles (e.g., ZIF-8 or UiO-66 analogs) known to create selective sub-nanochannels. Hollow fibers will be fabricated via a dry-jet wet spinning process, systematically varying dope composition (polymer/solvent/non-solvent/MOF loading), bore fluid chemistry, air-gap distance, and coagulation bath conditions. Fibers will be characterized for morphology (SEM), mechanical integrity, and pure-gas (CO2, CH4, N2) permeation properties using a constant-volume/variable-pressure apparatus.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable fabrication pathway for transforming novel channel-forming polymers into practical hollow fiber modules. It will provide critical structure-processing-property relationships linking spinning conditions to the preservation of selective nanochannels in the final fiber morphology, directly bridging advanced materials discovery and industrial membrane manufacturing.
proposals.novelty: While MOF-polymer composites have been studied for gas separation, the targeted scaling into hollow fibers specifically designed to preserve the function of engineered angstrom-scale ion or gas transport channels, combining Freeman group's expertise in sorption, polymer design, and membrane transport, is a novel and non-trivial engineering advancement beyond typical mixed-matrix membrane research.

proposals.title: Metal-Organic Framework Nanosheet-Polymer Composite Membranes with Tailored Sub-Nanometer Channels for Simultaneously High CO2 Permeability and CO2/O2 Selectivity
proposals.motivation: Efficient separation of CO2 from O2 is critical for life support in confined environments (e.g., spacecraft, submarines) and for reducing energy penalties in combustion processes. Conventional polymer membranes face a well-documented upper-bound tradeoff, where high CO2 permeability comes at the cost of low CO2/O2 selectivity, and vice-versa. New materials that invert this tradeoff are urgently needed to enable compact, energy-efficient separation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By incorporating two-dimensional metal-organic framework (MOF) nanosheets featuring well-defined, angstrom-scale pores into a high-free-volume polymer matrix, we can create hybrid membranes with molecular-sieving channels that simultaneously achieve high CO2 permeability and high CO2/O2 selectivity, surpassing the 2019 Robeson upper bound.
proposals.method: We will synthesize 2D MOF nanosheets (e.g., ZIF-7, ZIF-8 derivatives) with pore apertures tuned between 3.0-4.0 Å via pre-2026 synthetic methods to selectively discriminate CO2 (kinetic diameter ~3.3 Å) from O2 (~3.5 Å). These nanosheets will be incorporated into polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) via solution casting. Membrane performance will be evaluated using pure and mixed gas (CO2/O2) permeation tests. Advanced characterization (XRD, TEM, positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy) will correlate pore structure, nanosheet orientation, and free volume properties with transport metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for creating composite membranes that decouple permeability and selectivity by introducing rigid, size-selective pathways within a permeable matrix. It will provide a new dataset of high-performance materials for CO2/O2 separation and advance the fundamental understanding of gas transport in hybrid systems containing discrete sub-nanochannels.
proposals.novelty: While MOF-polymer composites are known, the deliberate use of 2D MOFs with pore sizes precisely at the angstrom scale to create a percolating network of molecular sieving channels within a polymer represents a novel architecture. This approach directly leverages Freeman group expertise in ion sub-nanochannels and salt sorption, applying it to the distinct challenge of gas pair separation with similar kinetic diameters.

proposals.title: Solvent-Free Fabrication of Oriented Polymer Hollow Fiber Membranes with Sub-Nanometer Channels for Selective Ion Transport
proposals.motivation: Hollow fiber membranes offer superior packing density and surface-area-to-volume ratios for industrial separations, but conventional fabrication relies on solvent-based processes with environmental and scalability drawbacks. Concurrently, recent advances in creating sub-nanometer, selective ion channels in flat-sheet membranes via solvent-free extrusion and orientation present a promising route for precise separations. Scaling this innovative channel-formation technology to a hollow fiber geometry would bridge a critical gap, enabling the deployment of advanced, energy-efficient ion-selective membranes in practical modules for water purification and resource recovery.
proposals.hypothesis: The solvent-free co-extrusion and post-extrusion orientation process, proven for creating tailored sub-nanometer channels in flat films, can be successfully adapted to produce hollow fiber membranes with analogous, radially oriented nanochannels, thereby imparting scalable, high-flux, and selective ion transport properties.
proposals.method: We will adapt a dual-layer, solvent-free co-extrusion system to produce hollow fibers, using a core polymer (e.g., polyethylene oxide) as a sacrificial template and a sheath of a model ion-conducting polymer (e.g., sulfonated polymers or polymers of intrinsic microporosity). Post-extrusion, controlled uniaxial or biaxial stretching will be applied to induce chain and pore orientation. The process will be characterized using in-line diameter monitoring, scanning electron microscopy, and X-ray diffraction. The resulting hollow fibers' ion transport properties (e.g., Na+/Mg2+ or Cl-/SO4^2- selectivity) and permeability will be evaluated using custom-built permeation cells, building on established protocols for salt sorption and transport analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational, scalable manufacturing platform for solvent-free hollow fiber membranes with designed nanochannels. It will provide the first structure-property-performance relationships for oriented sub-nanometer channels in a hollow fiber geometry, directly contributing to the development of next-generation membranes for selective ion separations, desalination, and electrochemical systems with improved module efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While solvent-free extrusion of flat-sheet membranes with oriented channels is an emerging area, its application to hollow fiber fabrication is unexplored. This proposal uniquely combines the geometric advantages of hollow fibers with the precise, solvent-free nanochannel engineering demonstrated in recent work on artificial ion channels and metal-organic framework composites.

proposals.title: Designing Polymers for Gas Separations by Maximizing Solubility-Selectivity: A Paradigm Shift from Size-Sieving
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 membrane research for gas separations (e.g., CO2/N2, CO2/CH4) has been dominated by the design of rigid, size-sieving polymers that minimize inter-chain spacing to enhance diffusion selectivity. This approach often leads to a well-documented permeability-selectivity trade-off (Robeson upper bound). An alternative strategy, prioritizing high gas solubility and solubility-selectivity, is underexplored for non-rubbery polymers, potentially offering a route to surpass current performance limits for solubility-differentiated gas pairs.
proposals.hypothesis: By deliberately designing polymers with high fractional free volume and specific chemical functionalities that maximize gas solubility, rather than minimizing void size, it is possible to achieve superior separation performance for gas pairs where solubility differences are significant, thereby circumventing traditional trade-offs.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of novel polymers (e.g., functionalized polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) and thermally rearranged (TR) polymer analogs) incorporating polar, quadrupole-interactive, or metal-coordinating groups (e.g., carboxylate, nitrile, or facilitated transport sites) to enhance condensable gas (CO2, H2S) solubility. Pure-gas sorption (via barometric or gravimetric techniques) and permeability measurements will be conducted to deconvolute solubility and diffusion coefficients. Molecular simulations (e.g., Grand Canonical Monte Carlo) will guide monomer selection by predicting gas sorption isotherms and analyzing polymer-gas interaction energies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete design framework for 'solubility-selective' polymers, providing quantitative structure-property relationships linking chemical functionality to gas solubility. It will expand the toolbox for membrane material design beyond size-sieving and demonstrate proof-of-concept membranes that leverage solubility for efficient separations, potentially redefining performance targets for specific gas pairs.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the prevailing design paradigm in high-performance polymer membranes from minimizing free volume element size to strategically engineering its chemical environment to maximize sorption, a concept not systematically pursued for glassy, microporous polymers. It combines Freeman group expertise in sorption and transport with novel polymer synthesis targeting specific gas-polymer interactions.

proposals.title: Ultrathin-Film Composite Membranes Based on Poly(ethylene oxide) Triacrylate for Enhanced Ion Selectivity and Permeance in Water Purification
proposals.motivation: Thin-film composite (TFC) membranes are critical for energy-efficient desalination and water treatment, but conventional polyamide layers face a fundamental trade-off between water permeance and salt rejection. Recent work, including Freeman's group's studies on polymers with precisely 3 ethylene oxide (EO) units, has shown exceptional ion-pair sorption and transport properties, suggesting a pathway to overcome this trade-off. Translating these promising materials into industrially relevant, defect-free ultrathin films (<50 nm) remains a significant fabrication challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a crosslinked polymer network based on a triacrylate monomer with exactly 3 EO units can be fabricated as an ultrathin selective layer (<30 nm) on a porous support, resulting in a TFC membrane with simultaneously high Na2SO4 rejection (>99%) and water permeance (>5 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) due to its sub-nanometer, ion-pair-selective transport channels.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the target poly(ethylene oxide) triacrylate (3 EO units) monomer and fabricate TFC membranes via a two-step process: (1) interfacial polymerization on a polysulfone ultrafiltration support, optimizing monomer concentration, reaction time, and solvent to control layer thickness, and (2) UV-initiated free-radical crosslinking to stabilize the network. Membrane characterization will include ellipsometry and TEM for thickness, ATR-FTIR for chemistry, and cross-flow filtration tests for permeance and salt rejection (NaCl, Na2SO4). Ion sorption will be quantified via quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation monitoring (QCM-D).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable fabrication route for next-generation TFC membranes with angstrom-scale, ion-selective pores derived from fundamental polymer design. It will provide the first industrial-scale performance data for this promising polymer class, directly linking its unique sorption properties, as studied in bulk films, to transport in ultrathin configurations. The results will guide the design of membranes for selective ion removal and resource recovery.
proposals.novelty: While the 3-EO polymer's bulk properties are known, its fabrication as a sub-30-nm selective layer for TFC membranes is unexplored. The approach uniquely combines precise polymer chemistry from fundamental studies with scalable interfacial polymerization, targeting industrial performance metrics rather than just model system characterization.

proposals.title: Electrochemically Active Metal-Organic Framework Membranes for Energy-Efficient, Selective Ion Pumping
proposals.motivation: Conventional membrane separations are fundamentally limited by passive diffusion, leading to a well-known trade-off between permeability and selectivity. For critical applications like lithium extraction or sulfate removal from brines, achieving high selectivity at low concentration gradients is energy-intensive. Pre-2026 work on sub-nanochannels and ion sorption has defined the equilibrium limits, but active transport mechanisms, which use external energy to drive specific species against their gradient, remain largely unexplored in synthetic membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating redox-active, ion-coordinating sites within the angstrom-scale pores of a metal-organic framework (MOF) membrane will enable the electrochemically controlled, active pumping of target ions (e.g., Li+, SO42-) by cyclically switching the oxidation state of the framework, thereby modulating ion affinity and transport direction.
proposals.method: We will synthesize thin-film composite membranes incorporating MOFs like UiO-66 or ZIF-8, functionalized with redox-active ligands (e.g., ferrocene, viologen derivatives). The membrane will serve as a working electrode in a custom electrochemical cell. Ion transport will be characterized under an applied potential waveform (cyclic voltammetry/chronoamperometry) using conductivity and ICP-MS measurements. We will quantify pumping flux, energy efficiency, and selectivity against competing ions (e.g., Na+/Li+, Cl-/SO42-), correlating performance with the pore chemistry and redox-switching kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design framework for non-equilibrium, active transport membranes, moving beyond the passive sorption-diffusion paradigm. It will provide quantitative metrics for energy efficiency in electrochemical pumping and demonstrate a new route to achieve ultra-high selectivity in challenging ionic separations, directly relevant to water treatment and resource recovery.
proposals.novelty: While MOFs have been studied for passive ion sieving, their use as electrochemically addressable, active pumps for ions is novel. This approach merges concepts from biological active transport with synthetic sub-nanochannel design, creating a new class of 'smart' membranes whose transport properties are dynamically controlled by an external stimulus.

proposals.title: Precise Ion Sieving via Scalable Polymer Membranes with Biomimetic, Metal-Organic Framework-Templated Subnanochannels
proposals.motivation: Achieving high ion selectivity and permeability in synthetic membranes remains a critical challenge for water purification, resource recovery, and energy technologies. Biological protein channels, like aquaporins, achieve exceptional performance through precisely defined pore geometries and chemistries, but their direct incorporation into membranes faces scalability and stability limitations. A synthetic, scalable platform that mimics the structural precision of biological channels is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding metal-organic framework (MOF) nanocrystals, which possess angstrom-scale, pre-defined pores, into a tailored polymer matrix will create scalable membranes with biomimetic, protein-like channels, enabling unprecedented combinations of ion selectivity and water flux based on precise size and chemical exclusion.
proposals.method: We will synthesize MOFs with pore apertures tuned to specific hydrated ion diameters (e.g., ZIF-8 for ~3.4 Å). These MOF nanocrystals will be functionalized with polymerizable ligands and co-polymerized with a hydrophilic, cross-linkable polymer matrix (e.g., poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate) to form defect-free, thin-film composite membranes. Ion transport properties (permeability, selectivity for Na+/K+/Mg2+/SO42-) will be characterized using diffusion cells and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, correlating performance with channel structure via spectroscopy and positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) for pore size analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, scalable pathway to engineer biomimetic subnanochannels within robust polymer membranes, moving beyond fragile protein-based systems. It will provide fundamental structure-property relationships linking MOF pore geometry and membrane matrix chemistry to ion sorption and transport, advancing the design of next-generation selective separations.
proposals.novelty: While MOFs have been studied as standalone channels, their use as sacrificial or integral, pore-defining templates within a continuous, processable polymer film to create a biomimetic membrane is novel. This approach uniquely decouples channel precision from matrix processability, a key limitation of prior bio-hybrid or purely synthetic subnanochannel designs.

proposals.title: Rigid, Angstrom-Precise Membranes via MOF-Polymer Composites for Selective Ion Separations
proposals.motivation: Achieving precise molecular separations, such as distinguishing between same-charge ions of similar size (e.g., K+ vs. Na+), requires membranes with rigid, uniform pores at the Angstrom scale. Current polymeric membranes often have flexible, polydisperse free-volume elements, limiting selectivity. Pre-2026 work on sub-nanochannels and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) demonstrates the potential for rigid, size-exclusion-based separations, but integrating these into scalable, defect-free membranes remains a key challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating MOFs with well-defined, rigid sub-nanometer channels into a high-performance polymer matrix will create composite membranes whose transport properties are dominated by the precise MOF pore architecture, enabling ultra-selective ion sieving based on precise size and hydration energy differences.
proposals.method: We will synthesize MOF nanocrystals (e.g., ZIF-8 with ~3.4 Å pores or custom MOFs with tuned apertures) and disperse them into a rigid, glassy polymer matrix (e.g., a polyimide or PIM). Membranes will be fabricated via solution casting and crosslinking to stabilize the interface and minimize non-selective pathways. Transport properties will be characterized using diffusion cell and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for salts like NaCl, KCl, and Na2SO4, explicitly accounting for ion-pairing effects as per recent group work to decouple pore-size exclusion from solution-diffusion mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework for designing composite membranes where selectivity is dictated by engineered, rigid nanopores rather than polymer segmental motion. It will provide fundamental insights into ion transport through confined, Angstrom-scale environments within a macroscopic membrane. The project will yield a new class of materials for challenging separations in water purification and resource recovery.
proposals.novelty: While MOF channels and polymer membranes are both studied, the novelty lies in strategically using MOFs not as additives for flux but as the primary, rigid sieving element within a polymer, creating a composite whose selectivity is directly programmed by the MOF's crystallographic pore dimensions, a concept not fully realized pre-2026.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Sensing via Ionic Current Modulation in Sub-1-nm Metal-Organic Framework/Polymer Composite Membranes
proposals.motivation: Single-molecule detection and sequencing technologies, such as nanopore-based DNA sequencing, are limited by the stochastic translocation dynamics and signal-to-noise ratio of biological or solid-state pores. A membrane-based platform with a dense, addressable array of identical, chemically tunable sub-nanometer channels could enable more controlled, multiplexed sensing of biomolecules and polymers. This addresses a critical pre-2026 need for high-throughput, low-cost analytical tools in proteomics and diagnostics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding oriented, angstrom-scale ion channels derived from two-dimensional metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) into an ultra-thin, defect-free polymer matrix will create a sensing membrane where the translocation of individual analyte molecules (e.g., oligonucleotides, peptides) through the MOF pores generates discrete, analyte-specific modulations in the transmembrane ionic current.
proposals.method: We will fabricate the sensing membrane by synthesizing oriented, sub-1-nm pore 2D MOF layers (e.g., ZIF-8 or analogous structures) via interfacial growth and transferring them onto a mechanically supportive, ultra-thin (sub-100 nm) polyamide film, leveraging Freeman group expertise in composite membranes. Ionic transport and single-molecule sensing properties will be characterized using a custom microfluidic cell with integrated Ag/AgCl electrodes, measuring current-time traces at applied biases. Data analysis will employ established nanopore signal processing algorithms (e.g., threshold detection, hidden Markov models) to classify translocation events based on current blockades and durations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials platform for solid-state single-molecule sensing, moving beyond single-pore devices to scalable membrane arrays. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay between confined ion transport, analyte-pore chemistry, and signal generation. The project will yield design rules for creating synthetic channels with tailored selectivity for specific analytes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the precise, modular pore chemistry of 2D MOFs—recently explored for ion separation—with ultra-thin polymer membranes to create a dense, parallel array of identical sensing elements, a significant departure from the stochastic fabrication of individual solid-state nanopores or the use of biological channels.

proposals.title: Roll-to-Roll Fabrication of Large-Area, Ion-Selective Membranes from 2D Material Nanosheets via Controlled Interlayer Spacing
proposals.motivation: Two-dimensional (2D) materials like graphene oxide (GO) offer unprecedented potential for creating sub-nanometer channels for precise ionic separations, as demonstrated in small-scale studies. However, the field lacks scalable, continuous manufacturing methods to produce large-area, defect-free membranes, which is the critical bottleneck preventing their industrial application in water purification and energy technologies. Developing such a process is essential to translate the exceptional transport properties observed at the lab scale into practical separation devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous, roll-to-roll (R2R) deposition process, coupled with in-situ chemical cross-linking and layer-by-layer stacking, can produce large-area, mechanically robust 2D material membranes with controlled, angstrom-scale interlayer spacing that dictates selective ion transport properties.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pilot-scale R2R slot-die coater to deposit aqueous dispersions of GO and other 2D nanosheets (e.g., MXene, MoS2) onto flexible polymer supports. The process will integrate a multi-stage drying zone with controlled humidity and temperature to regulate nanosheet assembly. In-line, we will implement a spray or vapor-phase cross-linking station using small molecule cross-linkers (e.g., diamines, borate) to fix the interlayer spacing and enhance mechanical integrity. Membrane properties (defect density, d-spacing via XRD, surface morphology) and performance (water permeability, salt rejection, ion selectivity) will be characterized and correlated with R2R processing parameters (coating speed, dispersion concentration, drying rate).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational, scalable manufacturing framework for 2D material membranes, moving beyond batch fabrication. It will provide a direct process-structure-property relationship for membranes made continuously, offering a roadmap for industrial scale-up. The resulting large-area membranes will be evaluated for challenging separations, such as mono/divalent ion selectivity, directly linking scalable fabrication to advanced transport performance.
proposals.novelty: While R2R is common for electronics, its application to fabricate 2D material membranes with precise sub-nanometer channel control for ionic separations is novel. The integration of in-situ chemical modification during continuous processing to lock in specific transport properties represents a significant advance over static, post-assembly treatments.

proposals.title: Molecular Dynamics Elucidation of Ion-Pairing and Competitive Sorption Effects on Trace Contaminant Transport in Polymeric Membranes
proposals.motivation: The design of next-generation membranes for water purification and resource recovery requires a fundamental understanding of how trace contaminants (e.g., heavy metals, oxyanions) interact with and transport through polymer matrices. Pre-2026 experimental work, including from our group, has shown that ion pairing significantly influences sulfate sorption in cation exchange membranes, but the atomistic-scale mechanisms governing these interactions, especially for complex, multi-ion mixtures, remain poorly understood. Molecular modeling is essential to bridge this knowledge gap and enable predictive membrane design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the free energy landscape for contaminant transport in hydrated polymers is dominated by specific, transient ion-pair and ion-polymer coordination events, and that molecular dynamics simulations can quantify these interactions to explain and predict experimentally observed selectivity and permeability.
proposals.method: We will employ all-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using GROMACS or LAMMPS with the OPLS-AA or CHARMM force fields for polymers and ions. Systems will include hydrated models of polymers relevant to Freeman's work (e.g., sulfonated poly(ether sulfone)). We will simulate multi-ion environments containing target contaminants (e.g., Pb2+, AsO43-) and competing background salts (NaCl, CaCl2). Key analyses will include potential of mean force (PMF) calculations via umbrella sampling to determine free energy barriers, radial distribution functions to identify coordination structures, and trajectory analysis to calculate diffusion coefficients and residence times.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomistic-scale view of how ion pairing and competitive solvation influence contaminant partitioning and diffusion in realistic, hydrated polymer membranes. It will deliver quantitative free energy profiles and mechanistic insights that directly inform the interpretation of sorption and transport data, moving membrane design from empirical tuning towards a physics-based paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While MD has been used to study salt transport, its targeted application to elucidate the molecular mechanisms of trace contaminant sorption and transport in ion-exchange polymers, explicitly accounting for ion-pairing effects identified in recent experimental work, is a novel and necessary step. This integrates Freeman's group's experimental expertise in sorption and sub-nanochannels with high-fidelity computational analysis.

proposals.title: Enhancing Membrane Stability in Aggressive Environments via Rigid Subnanoporous Metal-Organic Framework Fillers in Crosslinked Polymer Matrices
proposals.motivation: Polymer membranes for water treatment and gas separation suffer from plasticization and performance loss when exposed to aggressive feed components like organic solvents, oils, or high-salinity brines, which cause swelling and alter sorption/transport properties. This degradation limits membrane lifespan and efficiency in critical applications such as produced water treatment and chemical separations. Developing materials that resist these effects is essential for advancing membrane technology for harsh industrial feeds.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating rigid, angstrom-scale ion channels derived from metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) into a chemically crosslinked polymer matrix will create composite membranes with superior resistance to swelling and plasticization by maintaining a stable pore architecture and limiting polymer chain mobility upon exposure to aggressive contaminants.
proposals.method: We will synthesize composite membranes by dispersing tailored, sodium-selective MOF crystals (inspired by recent subnanochannel work) into a crosslinkable polyamide or polyimide matrix. Membrane properties will be characterized using sorption experiments with aggressive organics (e.g., toluene) and concentrated sulfate salts, analyzing ion pairing effects via techniques from recent CEM studies. Transport and stability will be evaluated by measuring water/salt permeability and mechanical properties before and after extended exposure to model aggressive feeds, comparing performance to pristine polymer controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for creating robust composite membranes by combining rigid, size-selective nanochannels with a crosslinked network to decouple selectivity from swelling. It will provide quantitative structure-property relationships linking filler geometry and matrix crosslinking density to resistance against specific contaminants. The findings will advance materials for separations in chemically challenging environments.
proposals.novelty: While MOF-polymer composites exist, the strategic use of rigid, subnanoscale MOF channels specifically to arrest the physical swelling of a crosslinked matrix upon contaminant sorption is a novel stabilization mechanism. This approach uniquely targets the combined mitigation of plasticization by organics and performance loss from multi-ion salt solutions within a single material platform.

proposals.title: Integrating Subnanometer-Selective Polymer Membranes into Microfluidic Architectures for Enhanced On-Chip Gas Exchange
proposals.motivation: Miniaturized gas processing, such as O2/CO2 control in portable medical devices or microscale chemical synthesis, is limited by the lack of efficient, selective gas exchange modules for lab-on-a-chip systems. Current microfluidic gas exchange relies on simple, non-selective permeable materials, offering poor selectivity and low throughput for critical gas pairs like O2/N2 or CO2/N2. Integrating advanced, selective membranes directly into chip-scale devices could enable transformative portable and point-of-care technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by fabricating ultra-thin, defect-free polymer membranes with subnanometer-scale, tailored transport channels and integrating them into optimized microfluidic architectures, we can achieve highly selective and permeable gas separation in a chip-scale format, surpassing the performance of conventional bulk membrane modules on a volumetric efficiency basis.
proposals.method: We will synthesize thin-film composite membranes using polymers with intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) and metal-organic framework (MOF) nanofillers to create tailored subnanometer channels, building on pre-2026 work on angstrom-scale ion channels. Membrane transport properties (permeability, selectivity) for O2, N2, and CO2 will be characterized using time-lag and mixed-gas sorption apparatus. These membranes will be integrated into custom-designed polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic chips using a sacrificial layer technique to create robust, leak-free membrane interfaces. Chip performance will be evaluated by measuring gas composition changes in flowing streams using integrated micro-sensors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design framework for incorporating high-performance gas-selective membranes into microfluidic devices. It will provide quantitative structure-property-performance relationships for subnanometer-channel membranes under microscale flow conditions, advancing the field of miniature chemical processing. The resulting prototype will demonstrate a significant step towards practical, membrane-based lab-on-a-chip gas exchangers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct convergence of Freeman group expertise in subnanometer-channel membrane design for ion transport with the engineering of microfluidic platforms for gas processing, a largely unexplored synergy. This moves beyond using membranes as simple passive layers to employing them as active, selective components engineered at the molecular scale for specific chip-based applications.

proposals.title: Probing the Limits of the Hydrocarbon-Centric Model: A Comparative Study of Gas Sorption and Transport in Perfluorinated versus Hydrocarbon Polymer Matrices
proposals.motivation: Fundamental models for gas sorption and transport in polymers, such as the dual-mode sorption model, are largely built on data from hydrocarbon-based polymers like PDMS. This has created a hydrocarbon-centric paradigm that may not accurately describe materials with radically different chemistries, such as perfluorinated polymers, which are critical for demanding membrane applications in gas separations and energy. A systematic comparison is needed to test the universality of these interaction models and inform the design of next-generation membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that perfluorinated polymer matrices will exhibit fundamentally different gas-polymer interaction parameters (e.g., solubility coefficients, Langmuir capacity, and diffusion activation energies) compared to their hydrocarbon analogs, due to their distinct polarizabilities and chemical inertness, challenging the predictive power of existing hydrocarbon-derived models for gas sorption.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of model perfluorinated polymers (e.g., poly(perfluoropropyl vinyl ether) or amorphous Teflon AF analogs) with controlled fractional free volume. Gas sorption isotherms (for CO2, CH4, N2, O2) will be measured using a high-pressure microbalance, and transport properties will be characterized via time-lag measurements. Data will be fitted to established models (dual-mode sorption, free volume theory), and the extracted parameters will be directly compared to benchmark hydrocarbon polymer (PDMS, PTMSP) data from the literature and our lab. Molecular dynamics simulations will be used to compute interaction energies and cavity size distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset and analysis contrasting gas interaction parameters in fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon polymer backbones. It will critically evaluate the transferability of existing sorption models, potentially leading to revised correlations or new predictive frameworks. The findings will directly guide material selection for gas separation membranes by clarifying the role of backbone fluorination.
proposals.novelty: While perfluorinated polymers are known for their chemical resistance, a deliberate, quantitative inversion of the polymer matrix from hydrocarbon to fluorocarbon to test the foundational assumptions of gas sorption models is a novel and systematic approach. It moves beyond incremental comparisons to challenge the core chemical premise of widely used predictive tools.

ideas.title: CO2-Responsive Membranes via Electrochromic Polymer Switching
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from the field of electrochromic smart windows, where materials like tungsten oxide change ion permeability and optical density in response to an electrical voltage. The analogous problem of controlling permeability with an external stimulus is solved here by using CO2 concentration (or the associated pH change) as the chemical trigger instead of voltage. The adapted research idea is to develop synthetic polymers that mimic this gating mechanism: the adsorption and reaction of CO2 will reversibly alter the polymer's redox state or ionic cross-link density, thereby creating membranes that autonomously change their permeability in response to ambient CO2 levels, useful for smart packaging, carbon capture, or controlled-release systems.

ideas.title: Microfluidic Screening Platform for CO2-Selective Protective Layers in H2/He Purification
ideas.description: This research develops a microfluidic membrane contactor array to rapidly screen the performance and degradation kinetics of novel CO2-selective protective layers for hydrogen or helium purification membranes. The chip-based platform enables parallel testing of multiple membrane material formulations and nanostructures under precisely controlled, minute gas mixtures and flow regimes, mimicking industrial feed conditions. This allows for the direct observation of CO2-induced poisoning mechanisms on the underlying H2/He-selective layer in real-time and the evaluation of the protective layer's efficacy. The core synergy is using microfluidics not just for separation studies, but as an accelerated materials discovery tool specifically for designing next-generation, multi-layered composite membranes with built-in resilience.

ideas.title: Cascaded Hybrid Systems: Decoupling Bulk Processing from Ultimate Purification
ideas.description: This approach decouples the challenges of throughput and ultimate purity by designing integrated separation cascades. High-permeability membranes or sorbents perform the initial bulk separation, concentrating the target stream for the final, ultra-selective stage. This architecture protects the expensive, highly selective material from fouling and minimizes its required surface area or volume. The system thus achieves the target ultra-high purity for critical applications while maintaining industrial-scale throughput and energy efficiency.

ideas.title: Investigate a hybrid membrane-absorption system with a biomimetic, facilitated transport membrane inspired by alveolar gas exchange
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to investigate a novel hybrid membrane-absorption system for CO2 capture by drawing an analogy from the highly efficient gas exchange in the human lung (alveoli). The analogous biological solution combines a thin, selective membrane with a liquid layer containing a chemical facilitator (hemoglobin) that reversibly binds target gases. Adapted to engineering, the research idea is to design a system where a thin, selective membrane is coupled with a flowing solvent containing a tailored, non-volatile reactive carrier (e.g., an amino acid salt or biomimetic compound) that specifically and reversibly binds CO2. This adaptation aims to synergize the selectivity of membranes with the high capacity and driving force maintenance of chemical absorption, where the facilitator in the solvent acts as a 'shuttle' to pull CO2 through the membrane and into the bulk solvent, potentially overcoming efficiency limits of standalone membrane or absorption processes.

ideas.title: Layer-by-Layer Engineered Ultrathin Membranes with Predictive Diffusivity-Functionality Maps
ideas.description: This research direction combines the predictive modeling of diffusivity tradeoffs (linked to free volume and hydration) with the architectural precision of layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly from optics. The core idea is to use LbL to fabricate composite selective layers where each nanoscale stratum is designed with specific polymer chemistry and interlayer spacing to create a controlled, graded profile of free volume and water content. By integrating these precisely engineered architectures with the fundamental transport models, we can generate predictive maps that link the layered structure to performance, allowing for the active programming of selectivity and permeability. This moves beyond empirical optimization to the de novo design of ultrathin membranes with tailored transport hierarchies for specific separations.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Discovery of Viscoelasticity-Tuned Polymers for CO2 Separation
ideas.description: This research direction employs high-throughput combinatorial synthesis and rapid testing—adapted from pharmaceutical discovery—to generate and evaluate vast libraries of polymers deliberately engineered to span a continuous spectrum of viscoelastic properties. Instead of screening pre-defined rubbery or glassy polymers, the platform systematically creates and tests hybrid materials with finely tuned intermediate dynamic mechanical properties, directly measuring their CO2 permeability and selectivity. This data-intensive approach will uncover non-linear relationships and performance maxima within the viscoelastic landscape, identifying novel material formulations that challenge the traditional dichotomy and optimize the trade-off between permeability and selectivity for carbon capture applications.

ideas.title: Ultra-Selective CO2 Delivery Membranes for Contaminant-Free Bioprocesses
ideas.description: This research direction develops composite membranes designed to solve the inverse problem of controllably introducing CO2 into greenhouses or bioreactors while prioritizing ultra-high selectivity over raw permeability. The membranes are engineered to allow moderate, controlled fluxes of CO2 but possess exceptional selectivity to rigorously exclude specific trace contaminants (e.g., ethylene from combustion sources, industrial VOCs, or microbial aerosols) that could disrupt sensitive plants, algae, or cell cultures. This transforms the CO2 delivery system from a simple gas adder into an integrated purification and dosing unit, guaranteeing the chemical and biological purity of the carbon input. The approach enables the use of lower-grade, impure CO2 sources (like flue gas or biogas) for high-value, purity-critical applications such as pharmaceutical algae cultivation or advanced plant phenotyping, without risking contamination.

ideas.title: Interface-Engineered Heterogeneous Membranes via Combinatorial Synthesis and In-Situ Characterization
ideas.description: This research program confronts the fundamental challenge of interfacial failure in composite membranes by making the interface the central design variable. A high-throughput combinatorial synthesis platform will fabricate libraries of membranes with systematically varied interfacial chemistries and architectures, focusing on covalently graded transitions between phases. Real-time, in-situ structural and mechanical characterization during synthesis and operation will directly link interfacial design to performance metrics like selectivity, flux, and durability. The outcome will be a set of validated design principles for creating robust, next-generation heterogeneous membranes where the interface is a performance-enhancing feature, not a fatal flaw.

ideas.title: Membrane-Based CO2 Delivery via Controlled Osmotic Pressure Gradients
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from controlled drug delivery systems to the inverse problem of adding CO2. In pharmaceutics, transdermal patches use rate-controlling membranes to passively deliver a steady drug dose via a concentration gradient. The adapted research idea is to design a membrane system to controllably add CO2 to greenhouses or bioreactors. A high-concentration CO2 source is placed on one side of a specifically engineered membrane, with the growth environment on the other. By tailoring the membrane's properties (material, thickness, permeability) and managing the CO2 partial pressure gradient, the system can passively meter a precise, scalable flux of CO2 into the enclosed space, providing a low-energy, controlled alternative to active injection and venting systems.

ideas.title: Apply High-Throughput Catalyst Discovery Methods to Polymer Screening for CO2 Separation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput experimentation (HTE) methodologies pioneered in heterogeneous catalyst discovery to the search for novel polymer membranes for CO2 separation. In catalysis, HTE platforms use automated synthesis robots to create vast libraries of solid catalyst formulations on wafer-like substrates, which are then rapidly tested for activity and selectivity in parallel microreactors. The adapted solution involves using similar automated dispensing systems (e.g., inkjet or syringe printers) to combinatorially deposit and cure polymer precursor 'inks'—varying monomers, crosslinkers, and functional additives—onto arrays of porous supports, creating a vast library of distinct membrane samples. A parallelized, miniaturized gas permeation testing station, using techniques like a time-lag apparatus or gas chromatography in a highly multiplexed format, would then rapidly screen each membrane spot for its CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity. This approach dramatically accelerates the discovery of next-generation polymer materials for carbon capture by evaluating thousands of formulations orders of magnitude faster than traditional sequential membrane fabrication and testing.

ideas.title: Topologically Optimized Asymmetric Membranes for Dynamic Biogas Upgrading
ideas.description: This research fuses topological optimization with biogas upgrading to create a new paradigm for membrane design. It involves developing multi-scale computational models that use topological optimization algorithms to generate 3D pore network blueprints specifically for CO2/CH4 separation, but with a key innovation: the optimization targets are not fixed. Instead, they are dynamically defined by real-time process conditions (e.g., variable CO2 concentration, feed pressure) to maximize overall system efficiency and methane recovery. The outcome is the fabrication of next-generation asymmetric membranes whose pore morphology is intricately engineered to provide optimal, context-dependent selectivity and permeability, leading to more robust and energy-efficient biogas purification plants.

ideas.title: Design glassy polymers with frustrated chain packing using bio-inspired rigid backbone motifs
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural biology and protein engineering, where rigid molecular scaffolds are used to create permanent, selective pores in synthetic ion channels. The analogous approach is to design glassy polymer backbones with intrinsically rigid, bulky, and contorted molecular motifs (such as spiro-centers, triptycenes, or ladder structures) that prevent dense, efficient chain packing. This 'frustrated packing' creates a stable network of interconnected microcavities within the polymer film, analogous to permanent molecular pores. The adapted research idea focuses on synthesizing and characterizing these polymers, aiming to simultaneously enhance CO2 permeability by providing ample diffusion pathways and maintain high CO2/CH4 selectivity by precisely engineering the size and chemical environment of the cavities through the choice of rigid backbone units.

ideas.title: Apply vascular network optimization from physiology to design membrane pore networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from physiology and biomechanics, where vascular networks are optimized for efficient fluid transport (e.g., via Murray's law and shear-stress-driven growth algorithms), to the design of asymmetric membrane pore networks. The analogous solution uses computational models that simulate the growth and pruning of network branches to minimize energy expenditure. Adapted to membrane science, this involves developing an algorithm that treats the membrane's pore network as a transport vasculature, using rules derived from target solute flux and pressure drop to topologically optimize the 3D pore morphology—branching from a selective skin layer into a macroporous support—thereby maximizing permeability and selectivity for specific separation processes.

ideas.title: Adapting Percolation Theory from Composite Conductivity to Model Diffusivity Tradeoffs
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to establish predictive models connecting the diffusivity tradeoff to polymer free volume and water content. By drawing an analogy from composite material science—specifically the modeling of electrical conductivity-mechanical strength tradeoffs using percolation theory—we can adapt a powerful framework. The analogous solution uses concepts of filler connectivity, matrix properties, and percolation thresholds to create structure-property maps. Adapted to polymer membranes, the research will model water content and polymer free volume as the dual components of a percolating network controlling diffusivity. The goal is to develop quantitative models that predict the diffusivity-selectivity tradeoff based on measurable parameters like hydration level and free volume, identifying critical percolation thresholds for designing next-generation polymer materials.

ideas.title: Adapting Non-Equilibrium Thermodynamics from Biophysics to Model Coupled Sorption-Diffusion
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption in porous media transport that sorption equilibria and Fickian diffusion are independent, sequential processes. The analogy is drawn from biophysics, where models of ion transport across cell membranes successfully abandoned the assumption of independent channel selectivity and permeability by employing non-equilibrium thermodynamics. The analogous solution uses coupled flux equations with cross-coefficients to capture how binding (sorption) dynamically influences mobility (diffusion). Adapting this solution, the research will develop a transport model for nanoporous materials (e.g., MOFs, zeolites) where sorption and diffusion are treated as coupled phenomena through a non-equilibrium thermodynamic framework. This will involve defining phenomenological coefficients that link sorption affinity to diffusional resistance, allowing the prediction of how competitive sorption or cooperative effects alter transport selectivity in ways current independent models cannot capture.

ideas.title: Develop structure-property relationships for polymer transport using Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationships (QSAR) from pharmacology
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in medicinal chemistry—predicting drug activity from molecular structure—to the challenge of linking polymer structure to transport properties. In pharmacology, Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) modeling successfully correlates molecular descriptors with biological activity to guide drug design. The adapted research idea applies this framework by defining quantitative descriptors for polymer chemical structures (e.g., repeat unit polarity, free volume, chain rigidity) and using machine learning to build predictive models that relate these descriptors to intrinsic water and ion transport metrics. This systematic, computational approach would enable the rational design of next-generation polymer membranes for applications like desalination, batteries, and fuel cells by predicting performance from chemical structure prior to synthesis.

ideas.title: Membrane-Mediated Waste-Heat Coupled CO2 Recapture and Delivery for Controlled Environments
ideas.description: This redesigned system targets the efficient, low-energy addition of CO2 by integrating a selective membrane not with ambient air, but with an on-site, dilute waste CO2 stream. The membrane module acts as a controllable interface between this exhaust and a liquid absorption loop, enabling precise regulation of CO2 capture. The energy required for absorbent regeneration is supplied primarily by coupling to low-grade waste heat from the exhaust source or the greenhouse heating system. This creates a closed-loop or semi-closed system that upgrades waste CO2 for fertilization use with minimal operational energy penalty, making it cost-competitive with conventional methods while improving sustainability.

ideas.title: Quantifying Sorption-Mediated Diffusivity via Coupled In Situ Spectroscopy and Dynamic Modeling
ideas.description: This research designs an experimental-theoretical framework to explicitly quantify how sorption kinetics modulate local diffusivity, moving beyond the independent assumption. A model membrane is probed using in situ infrared spectroscopy and quartz crystal microgravimetry to track spatially and temporally resolved penetrant concentration and swelling, providing direct data on sorption dynamics. These results feed a continuum transport model where local diffusivity is a function of the instantaneous sorption state and its rate of change. The validated coupled model is then tested by predicting separation performance for novel gas mixtures, providing a rigorous, falsifiable benchmark for the coupled mechanism hypothesis.

ideas.title: Optimize biogas upgrading membranes using a multi-stage, feedback-controlled cascade inspired by isotope separation
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of optimizing membrane systems for biogas upgrading by drawing an analogy from the field of nuclear engineering, specifically isotope separation via gas centrifuge cascades. The analogous solution uses a multi-stage, feedback-controlled cascade with recycle streams to achieve high-purity separation efficiently. The adapted idea proposes to develop an integrated, multi-stage membrane cascade process for purifying methane from CO2 in biogas. This system would employ strategic recycling of permeate and retentate streams between stages, coupled with real-time sensors and automated controls, to dynamically optimize operating conditions. This approach aims to significantly enhance methane purity and recovery rates while maintaining energy efficiency, especially when dealing with fluctuating biogas feedstocks.

ideas.title: Adopt Layer-by-Layer Assembly from Thin-Film Optics, Inspired by Biomimetic Mineralization
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of adopting layer-by-layer assembly from thin-film optics by drawing an analogy from biomimetic mineralization. In nature, organisms use organic templates to direct the precise layer-by-layer growth of inorganic crystals, creating robust composite structures like nacre. The analogous solution is adapted by using alternating layers of organic polyelectrolytes and inorganic precursors during the assembly process. The organic layers serve as structured templates to direct the nucleation, crystallization, and interfacial bonding of the inorganic selective layer material (e.g., MOFs, zeolites, or metal oxides). This results in an ultrathin, composite selective membrane with a controlled, interpenetrating organic-inorganic architecture, engineered to enhance molecular selectivity, mechanical integrity, and resistance to fouling or degradation.

ideas.title: Adapting Free Energy Perturbation from Drug Discovery to Simulate Gas-Polymer Binding Affinities
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using molecular dynamics simulations from biophysics to simulate gas diffusion and sorption in polymers. By drawing an analogy to computational drug discovery, we can adapt a more advanced and quantitative technique. In drug discovery, Free Energy Perturbation (FEP) simulations are used to calculate the relative binding affinities of different drug candidates to a protein. This analogous solution can be adapted to the context of gas-polymer interactions. The adapted research idea is to use FEP calculations within molecular dynamics simulations to compute the relative sorption free energies of various gas molecules in a polymer matrix. By performing alchemical transformations that convert one gas molecule into another (e.g., CO2 to N2) while it is solvated in the polymer, we can precisely predict the polymer's thermodynamic selectivity, providing atomic-scale insights crucial for designing next-generation separation membranes or gas storage materials.

ideas.title: Mechanistic, Multi-Timescale Molecular Dynamics Framework for Predicting CO2 Plasticization and Material Evolution in Glassy Polymers
ideas.description: This research integrates advanced molecular dynamics techniques from biophysics—such as enhanced sampling and coarse-graining—with polymer physics to create a predictive, multi-scale simulation framework. It explicitly models the time-dependent process where initial CO2 sorption swells the polymer matrix, increasing chain mobility and free volume, which in turn accelerates further CO2 diffusion and sorption, leading to a runaway plasticization effect. The key innovation is simulating this entire dynamic cascade at atomic detail to identify specific polymer structural motifs and chain dynamics that resist or mitigate the plasticization feedback loop. The outcome will be a validated computational tool to design next-generation glassy polymers with inherently stable performance for long-term CO2 separation applications.

ideas.title: Closed-loop active discovery of polymer transport mechanisms through integrated synthesis, simulation, and machine learning
ideas.description: This project establishes an autonomous platform that combines robotic synthesis of diverse polymer libraries with high-throughput characterization of water and ion transport properties. A co-evolutionary active learning loop uses this experimental data to train and validate high-fidelity molecular dynamics simulations, which in turn propose new candidate structures to test, strategically exploring the chemical space. The core outcome is a fundamentally causal, mechanism-based model that disentangles the specific chemical motifs and morphological features governing selective transport, moving beyond statistical correlation to enable truly predictive design of next-generation polymer membranes.

ideas.title: Closed-loop modeling and accelerated aging platform for CO2-induced plasticization and synergistic degradation in glassy polymers
ideas.description: This research creates a tightly coupled system where predictive models for CO2 plasticization are not developed in isolation but are continuously validated and refined against experimental data from long-term performance under realistic conditions. An accelerated aging platform subjects candidate polymer films and membranes to cyclic CO2 exposure, temperature swings, and mechanical load, while using in-situ techniques like spectroscopic ellipsometry and dynamic mechanical analysis to monitor physical aging and property changes. The data directly tests model predictions and reveals unforeseen failure modes, enabling the iterative development of models that accurately predict not just pure plasticization but also its interplay with physical aging and creep. This closed-loop approach yields both robust predictive tools and experimentally vetted design rules for materials stable over their intended operational lifetime.

ideas.title: Predicting CO2-Induced Polymer Plasticization via Pharmacokinetic Compartmental Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a parallel problem in pharmacology—predicting the dynamic effect of a substance (a drug) on a complex system (the human body)—to model CO2-induced plasticization in glassy polymers. In pharmacology, pharmacokinetic compartmental models successfully predict the time-dependent concentration and effect of a drug by modeling transfer between physiological compartments. Here, we propose developing an analogous predictive model where the polymer microstructure (e.g., dense glassy domains, interfacial regions, pre-existing free volume elements) is represented as a set of kinetic compartments. CO2 sorption, diffusion, and swelling are modeled as transfer processes between these compartments with specific rate constants. The 'pharmacodynamic' component of the model links local CO2 concentration to a reduction in glass transition temperature and modulus, enabling the prediction of transient plasticization effects. This approach moves beyond equilibrium models to guide the design of materials with stable performance under dynamic CO2 exposure by identifying and tailoring the kinetic compartments most responsible for undesirable softening.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Enhanced Sampling Framework for Gas Transport in Polymers
ideas.description: This research overcomes the timescale limitation of conventional MD by implementing a hybrid simulation framework. It uses enhanced sampling techniques like metadynamics within all-atom simulations to compute free energy barriers and pathways for gas diffusion. These results then parameterize higher-level coarse-grained models capable of simulating the full sorption and diffusion process over experimentally relevant timescales. The multi-scale approach is systematically validated against experimental permeability coefficients to ensure predictive accuracy for material design.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Screening of Polymer Transport Properties via Materials Genome Initiative Framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from metallurgy and alloy design under the Materials Genome Initiative (MGI), which integrated high-throughput computational screening with rapid experimental synthesis and characterization to accelerate alloy discovery. The analogous solution involved using density functional theory on compositional libraries and combinatorial deposition to map alloy properties. Adapted to polymer research, the idea proposes an integrated pipeline: first, screen a vast virtual polymer library (varying repeat units, architectures, blends) using coarse-grained simulations or group contribution methods to predict transport properties (e.g., permeability, selectivity). The top computational predictions then direct a focused experimental campaign using automated synthesis robots and combinatorial formulation (e.g., inkjet printing of monomer gradients) coupled with high-speed characterization (e.g., gradient film permeability mapping) to rapidly validate and refine the structure-transport relationships, dramatically accelerating the discovery of polymers for separation membranes or battery electrolytes.

ideas.title: Modular, replaceable membrane liner cartridges for integrated reactor and pipeline systems
ideas.description: This system uses precision-engineered, modular membrane cartridges designed to be installed as a continuous internal liner within standard reactors or pipelines. The structural integrity of the vessel is maintained by the existing wall, which now acts as a pressure shell and support. The cartridge can be removed and replaced through access ports or during routine maintenance without damaging the host structure, enabling membrane-specific upgrades, cleaning, or repair while preserving the capital investment in the reactor or pipeline itself.

ideas.title: Adaptive Membrane Systems with Neuromorphic Control for Fluctuating Feeds
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from computational neuroscience and neuromorphic engineering to the challenge of designing membranes and processes for feeds with fluctuating pressure or composition. The analogous field solves the problem of processing dynamic, non-stationary inputs using event-driven, adaptive spiking neural networks that respond efficiently to change rather than operating at a constant pace. The adapted idea is to design adaptive membrane systems with a 'neuromorphic' control architecture. These systems would treat fluctuations in feed conditions as asynchronous events, triggering specific, localized physical or chemical adaptations within the membrane module—such as real-time adjustment of local flow resistance, active surface property modulation, or segment isolation—to maintain separation efficiency and stability without assuming constant feed parameters.

ideas.title: Modular, fault-tolerant membrane arrays inspired by satellite solar panel deployment
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from aerospace engineering, specifically the deployment of modular, fault-tolerant solar panel arrays on satellites, to the challenge of scaling up membrane units for shipboard CO2 capture. The analogous satellite solution uses compact, interconnected, and redundantly wired panels that deploy reliably in the extreme environment of space. Adapted to the maritime context, the research would develop compact, robust membrane systems composed of standardized, swappable membrane cartridge units. These cartridges would be integrated into a foldable, rack-like array that can be stowed compactly for installation and then deployed within the constrained space of a ship's exhaust system. The design would include redundant gas flow pathways, ensuring the system remains operational if individual membrane modules become fouled or damaged, thereby enhancing overall robustness and scalability for maritime exhaust gas treatment.

ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fatal throughput limitation of pure layer-by-layer assembly by combining scalable and rapid deposition techniques with localized precision engineering. A high-speed process first creates a nanostructured base layer over large or complex areas, establishing the primary morphology and bulk material properties. Subsequently, a minimal, optimized number of layer-by-layer cycles are applied only where critical interfacial control is needed, such as at the surface or within specific pore architectures, to fine-tune selectivity and stability. This hybrid system decouples scale from precision, enabling the fabrication of architecturally controlled composite layers for practical, large-scale applications.

This approach maintains the benefits of modular deployment while ensuring robust performance across diverse and fluctuating industrial conditions.

ideas.title: Deploy Plug-and-Play Membrane Units with Centralized Regeneration Hubs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from distributed telecommunications infrastructure to the problem of scaling carbon capture for distributed industrial sources. In telecoms, the 'small cell' model solved the scaling problem by deploying numerous standardized, low-power units for local signal handling, connected to centralized hubs for core processing. The adapted research idea proposes deploying ultra-compact, skid-mounted membrane units as 'capture cells' at numerous small industrial sites. These units would perform the initial capture and concentration of CO2. Instead of each unit having its own energy-intensive regeneration system, they would be linked—via a scheduled collection service or local pipeline network—to a shared, centralized regeneration facility. This hub would service a cluster of capture units, efficiently regenerating the membranes and processing the concentrated CO2 stream. This approach decouples the scalable, modular deployment of capture points from the significant capital and operational cost of regeneration, enabling rapid scale-up for distributed sources while maintaining economic viability through shared infrastructure.
